channel.title: Posts on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Alien Truth Social

item.title: UFO photographs
item.description:
Since the 1940s, media has documented numerous purported photographs of Unidentified Flying Objects (UFOs), many of which have been identified as hoaxes. Professor Branden W. Joseph has noted a distinct genre of UFO photography, often characterized by blurry, saucer-like forms with ambiguous scale. Notable photographic incidents include the “flying disc craze” of 1947 with photographs like those of Frank Ryman and Albert Weaver, the Rhodes UFO photographs from Arizona, and the widely recognized McMinnville UFO photographs from Oregon. Later examples include the Mariana film from 1950, the Lubbock Lights in 1951, and the Passaic photographs in 1952. The article also details alleged UFO sightings and photographic evidence from the late 20th century, including the Billy Meier hoaxes, the Hudson Valley sightings, the Belgian hoax, and the Calvine photograph. More recently, the Phoenix Lights in 1997 and encounters documented by Navy pilots in 2004 and 2015 have been presented with photographic and video evidence.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_photographs

item.title: "CARET" Drone Incident
item.description:
The “CARET” Drone Incident involved alleged sightings and photographs of small, unidentified aircraft in California and Lake Tahoe, Nevada, between May and June 2007. These sightings gained significant media attention, with some calling the photographs the clearest UFO images since the Gulf Breeze incident. However, in 2023, an artist claimed to have created the “alien language” and 3D models used in the incident, stating it was a hoax orchestrated for monetary gain. Investigations by VFX artists and others suggested the photos were computer-generated, and later research located where the photos were actually taken, contradicting the original claims.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%22CARET%22_Drone_Incident

item.title: UFO religion
item.description:
A UFO religion, also known as a UFO cult, is a religion where belief in extraterrestrial entities and communication with them is central. These religions often posit that aliens are interested in humanity’s welfare and can help solve Earth’s problems with their advanced technology and spirituality. Their origins are linked to science fiction, ufology, and UFO sighting narratives. Some historians consider the Aetherius Society or the “I AM” Activity to be the first UFO religions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_religion

item.title: The Evolution of UFO Disclosure Advocacy in the United States
item.description:
The disclosure movement is a social movement advocating for governments to declassify information about UFOs and “non-human intelligence.” Adherents believe this information could benefit humanity and often predict a future event called “disclosure” when these secrets will be revealed. Notable figures associated with the movement include Steven Greer, David Wilcock, and Luis Elizondo. The movement encompasses various beliefs about the origin of UFOs, including extraterrestrial, interdimensional, or even time-traveling sources.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disclosure_movement

item.title: United States UFO files
item.description:
The United States UFO files, also known as the UFO files or UAP files, are a collection of declassified U.S. government records concerning UFOs and unidentified anomalous phenomena. Released by the Trump administration starting on May 8, 2026, through the Presidential Unsealing and Reporting System for UAP Encounters (PURSUE) website, these files include reports, photographs, videos, and witness accounts. The initial release contained materials dating from 1944 to recent years, following increased government and congressional attention to UFOs. The released materials did not confirm the existence of extraterrestrial life, with the Pentagon describing them as unresolved cases and some experts suggesting they could be explained by mundane phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_UFO_files

item.title: Cryptoterrestrial hypothesis
item.description:
The cryptoterrestrial hypothesis suggests that UFO sightings are evidence of a hidden, technologically advanced civilization originating from Earth. This idea has roots in late 19th-century literature, with authors like Jules Verne and H.G. Wells exploring subterranean or hidden races. The concept gained traction in the mid-20th century through science fiction magazines and later became a subject of discussion among UFO researchers. The term “crypto-terrestrial” itself was coined in the 2000s to describe theoretical indigenous humanoids.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptoterrestrial_hypothesis

item.title: British UFO Research Association
item.description:
The British UFO Research Association (BUFORA) is a UK organization dedicated to investigating UFO phenomena in the British Isles. Founded in 1964, it reportedly had around 1,000 members in 1997 and investigates over 400 cases annually, with 95% identified as hoaxes. BUFORA has held annual conferences since 1987 and supports witnesses who believe they have encountered extraterrestrials. The organization has also been critical of the UK government’s handling of UFO-related information under the Freedom of Information Act.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_UFO_Research_Association

item.title: Operação Prato
item.description:
Operation Plate was an investigation conducted by the Brazilian Air Force between 1977 and 1978 in response to numerous UFO sightings on the island of Colares, Pará. Local residents reported being harmed by lights in the sky, which they called “Chupa Chupa.” The operation, commanded by Captain Uyrangê Hollanda, concluded after four months without finding any unusual phenomena. Conspiracy theories later emerged, particularly after Captain Hollanda’s death, with some ufologists claiming the lights caused injuries consistent with radiation or that they sucked blood from people.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Prato

item.title: UFO conspiracy theories
item.description:
UFO conspiracy theories posit that governments worldwide, particularly the United States, suppress evidence of extraterrestrial or non-human intelligence controlling UFOs. These theories gained traction in the 1950s with authors like Donald Keyhoe and gained further momentum through popular culture, including films and television shows. While mainstream science and skeptics find little verifiable proof for these claims, they persist, often incorporating elements like cattle mutilations, alien abductions, and secret government programs.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_conspiracy_theories

item.title: UFO Report (U.S. Intelligence)
item.description:
Preliminary Assessment: Unidentified Aerial Phenomena, also known as the UAP Report, is a federally mandated assessment published by the Office of the Director of National Intelligence on June 25, 2021. The report summarized information regarding unidentified aerial phenomena (UAPs), including unidentified flying objects (UFOs), and identified national security and pilot safety concerns. The assessment analyzed 144 observations of UAPs by U.S. Armed Forces personnel between 2004 and 2021, finding that the causes of most observations could not be identified. The report proposed five potential explanatory categories for these phenomena, including airborne clutter, natural atmospheric phenomena, U.S. government or foreign adversary systems, and an “other” category.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Report_%28U.S._Intelligence%29

item.title: NASA Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena Independent Study Team
item.description:
NASA’s Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena Independent Study Team (UAPIST) was formed in 2022 to recommend a roadmap for analyzing UAPs. The team, chaired by David Spergel, focused on unclassified data and how to effectively analyze information from various sources. Their final report, released in September 2023, found no evidence of extraterrestrial life related to UAP sightings. The study recommended a rigorous, evidence-based approach to UAP research, including the use of artificial intelligence and citizen observers.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASA_Unidentified_Anomalous_Phenomena_Independent_Study_Team

item.title: National UFO Reporting Center
item.description:
The National UFO Reporting Center (NUFORC) is a nonprofit corporation in Washington State that documents UFO and UAP sightings. Founded in 1974, it has cataloged nearly 170,000 reported sightings, primarily in the United States. The center provides statistics, graphs, and maps to aid in research and offers a 24-hour hotline and online form for submitting reports. Peter Davenport has served as director since 1994, and the organization is largely funded by Davenport and Christian Stepien.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_UFO_Reporting_Center

item.title: Project Blue Book
item.description:
Project Blue Book was the United States Air Force’s systematic study of unidentified flying objects (UFOs) from March 1952 to December 1969. Headquartered at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, the project aimed to determine if UFOs posed a threat to national security and to scientifically analyze UFO-related data. While thousands of reports were collected and analyzed, the project concluded that no UFOs investigated indicated a threat to national security, nor did they represent technological advancements beyond modern scientific knowledge or extraterrestrial vehicles. The project was terminated following the Condon Report, which suggested that UFO studies were unlikely to yield major scientific discoveries.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Project_Blue_Book

item.title: Rendlesham Forest incident
item.description:
The Rendlesham Forest incident involved reported sightings of unexplained lights near Rendlesham Forest in Suffolk, England, in December 1980. USAF personnel claimed to have seen a UFO, and the event is often called “Britain’s Roswell.” Sceptics suggest the sightings were misinterpretations of a fireball, the Orfordness Lighthouse, and bright stars. The UK Ministry of Defence stated the event posed no threat to national security and was not investigated as such.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rendlesham_Forest_incident

item.title: The Galileo Project
item.description:
The Galileo Project is an international scientific research initiative launched in 2021 to search for extraterrestrial intelligence and technology, and to investigate anomalous Unidentified Flying Objects (UFOs) and Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (UAP). Led by Harvard astrophysicist Avi Loeb, the project aims to gather new data on UFOs using dedicated sensor systems and make this data publicly available for scientific scrutiny. It also focuses on searching for interstellar objects and non-manmade artificial satellites. The project has faced both praise and skepticism from the scientific community regarding its methods and claims.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Galileo_Project

item.title: National Investigations Committee On Aerial Phenomena
item.description:
The National Investigations Committee On Aerial Phenomena (NICAP) was a UFO research organization active in the United States from 1956 to 1980. Despite facing financial difficulties, NICAP gained significant visibility and respectability, advocating for scientific investigation of UFO sightings and arguing for a governmental cover-up of UFO evidence. The organization’s membership fluctuated, peaking in the early 1960s before declining sharply after the publication of the Condon Report. NICAP officially dissolved in 1980, and its archives were later acquired by the Center for UFO Studies.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Investigations_Committee_On_Aerial_Phenomena

item.title: The Truth About Flying Saucers: A Special Report
item.description:
The article “Have We Visitors From Space?” published in Life magazine on April 7, 1952, strongly supported the extraterrestrial hypothesis for UFOs. It detailed ten UFO incidents deemed “most credible” by the authors, including sightings by astronomers and military personnel. The piece quoted experts like rocket scientist Walther Johannes Riedel and physicist Maurice Anthony Biot, who suggested an “out-of-world basis” for the observed phenomena. The article’s publication is believed to have contributed to the 1952 UFO flap, a subsequent wave of reports that summer.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Have_We_Visitors_From_Space%3F

item.title: Roswell, New Mexico
item.description:
Roswell, New Mexico, is a city in Chaves County with a population of 48,422 as of the 2020 census. It is known for its association with the 1947 Roswell incident, which has led to a tourism industry centered on ufology and alien-themed attractions. The city also has a history in rocketry, with exhibits dedicated to Robert H. Goddard’s work. Roswell’s economy includes agriculture, manufacturing, and distribution, and it has a history of minor league baseball.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roswell%2C_New_Mexico

item.title: Jeffry Shearer Rense: American Internet Radio Host and Conspiracy Theorist
item.description:
Jeff Rense is an American internet radio talk-show host known for propagating conspiracy theories. His website and podcast cover topics such as 9/11 conspiracies, ufology, the paranormal, chemtrails, and alternative medicine. Rense’s work has been described as pro-Nazi and antisemitic by the Anti-Defamation League and the Southern Poverty Law Center. He began his on-air experience in the 1970s while a student at the University of California, Santa Barbara.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeff_Rense

item.title: Galactic Hoax: The Ashtar Command Message
item.description:
On November 26, 1977, a broadcast signal intrusion occurred in southern England, where the audio of a Southern Television broadcast was replaced by a voice claiming to be Vrillon of the Ashtar Galactic Command. The message urged humanity to abandon weapons and live in peace for a “future awakening” and “higher state of evolution.” Investigations revealed the Hannington transmitter had rebroadcast a signal from an unauthorized nearby transmitter. This event prompted numerous calls from the public and widespread media coverage, with some reports offering contradictory details about the speaker and their message. The incident later became a footnote in ufology, with some questioning the explanation of a transmitter hijack.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southern_Television_broadcast_interruption

item.title: Die Glocke (conspiracy theory)
item.description:
Die Glocke, meaning “The Bell,” is a purported secret Nazi scientific device from the 1940s. It is described as a wonder weapon or experimental device, allegedly involving a radioactive liquid and antigravity or plasma technology. Claims about Die Glocke originated with Polish author Igor Witkowski and were popularized by military journalist Nick Cook, who linked it to Nazi occultism. Mainstream reviewers have largely dismissed these claims as pseudoscientific, recycled rumors, or a hoax.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Die_Glocke_%28conspiracy_theory%29

item.title: Cosmic Circle of Fellowship
item.description:
The Cosmic Circle of Fellowship (CCF) was a UFO religion founded in Chicago in 1954 by William R. Ferguson, Edward A. Surine, and Edna I. Valverde. Ferguson, a UFO contactee, claimed to be in contact with a Martian being named Khauga and wrote a book about these claims, which the CCF later published. The group believed in worshiping extraterrestrial beings and incorporated elements of Christian religiosity, believing they received messages from both aliens and Jesus. Members practiced trance-channeling to receive messages from these higher beings, which were then studied.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmic_Circle_of_Fellowship

item.title: Bob Lazar
item.description:
Robert Scott Lazar claims he worked on reverse-engineering extraterrestrial technology at a classified site near Area 51. His accounts of his education and employment history are disputed, with universities and institutions having no record of his claimed affiliations. Lazar was convicted of pandering in 1990, and his company faced probation in 2006 for chemical sales violations. Despite these issues, his claims have significantly increased public attention on Area 51 and fueled conspiracy theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bob_Lazar

item.title: Unidentified flying object
item.description:
An unidentified flying object (UFO), also known as an unidentified aerial phenomenon (UAP), is an object or phenomenon observed in the sky that cannot be immediately identified. While unusual sightings have been reported for centuries, UFOs gained cultural prominence after World War II. Investigations by governments and organizations have generally identified most UFOs as known objects or atmospheric phenomena, though a small percentage remain unexplained. The term “UFO” was coined by Captain Edward J. Ruppelt for the USAF to encompass a broader range of shapes than the earlier term “flying saucer.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_flying_object

item.title: Crop Circles: A Comprehensive Overview
item.description:
A crop circle, also known as a crop formation or corn circle, is a pattern created by flattening crops, typically cereal. The term was coined in the early 1980s, and while fringe theorists suggest obscure natural or alien origins, all crop circles are consistent with human causation. In 1991, Doug Bower and Dave Chorley claimed responsibility for creating over 200 crop circles in England, leading to a substantial increase in reports. These formations often appear overnight and are frequently found near roads, populated areas, and historical landmarks in the UK.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crop_circle

item.title: Baltic Sea Anomaly: Mysterious Circle Explained
item.description:
The Baltic Sea anomaly is a feature observed on a sonar image taken in 2011 by the OceanX diving team while treasure hunting. The team suggested the image depicted an object of unusual, possibly non-natural origin, leading to tabloid speculation about a sunken UFO. However, a consensus of experts and scientists believe the image most likely represents a natural geological formation. Analysis of rock samples from the site indicated they are common geological materials, and the sonar image itself has been criticized for distortions rendering it unreliable.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baltic_Sea_anomaly

item.title: Identification studies of UFOs
item.description:
Identifying UFOs is challenging due to the poor quality of evidence, with many sightings attributed to misidentifications of natural phenomena, aircraft, or other prosaic explanations. Major studies, such as Project Blue Book and the French GEPAN/SEPRA investigation, have found that a significant majority of reported UFOs can be explained. Common causes for misidentification include astronomical objects like Venus and meteors, as well as balloons, aircraft, and atmospheric phenomena like mirages.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Identification_studies_of_UFOs

item.title: Demonic UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The demonic UFO hypothesis proposes that unidentified flying object (UFO) sightings are caused by demonic influence or are demons themselves. This interpretation suggests that cultural and religious frameworks shape how anomalous aerial phenomena are understood, with modern interpretations shifting from angelic to alien, and now to demonic. Various individuals, including psychologists, theologians, occultists, evangelists, authors, and even a Vice President, have explored or endorsed this hypothesis. Prominent figures who have discussed this idea include Tim Lomas, Brendan Case, Marjorie Cameron, Walter Vinson Grant Sr., Gordon Creighton, Clifford Wilson, John Weldon, Zola Levitt, Seraphim Rose, Hal Lindsey, W.T. Widman, Nick Redfern, Luis Elizondo, Harold E. Puthoff, and JD Vance.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demonic_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Cattle mutilation
item.description:
Cattle mutilation, characterized by the unusual and often bloodless killing of cattle with specific body parts removed, began to be reported in the late 1960s and continued into the 1980s. While many such incidents are attributed to natural decomposition and predation, some evidence suggests an organized effort, with theories ranging from cults and aliens to covert government operations. Investigations have explored possibilities such as secret weapons testing or monitoring for public health threats, while conventional explanations often point to natural causes and animal scavenging.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cattle_mutilation

item.title: The United Nuwaubian Nation
item.description:
The Nuwaubian Nation, also known as the Nuwaubian movement or United Nuwaubian Nation, is an American religious organization founded by Dwight York. Scholars of religion have characterized it as a new religious movement and a black nationalist group with eclectic and evolving beliefs. York initially claimed to be the grandson of Muhammad Ahmad, later asserting he was an extraterrestrial named Yaanuwn. The group’s teachings are materialistic, dismissing a spiritual realm, and emphasize race within a black nationalist framework, viewing white people as having a fundamentally separate origin. The movement is millenarian, with prophecies of an apocalypse and the salvation of the righteous.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuwaubian_Nation

item.title: Raëlism
item.description:
Raëlism is a UFO religion founded in the 1970s by Claude Vorilhon, also known as Raël. The religion teaches that humanity was created by extraterrestrials called the Elohim, who have been mistaken for gods throughout history. Raëlists believe that prophets like Buddha, Jesus, and Muhammad were Elohim-human hybrids, with Raël being the final prophet. The movement advocates for scientific advancement, particularly in human cloning, and promotes a liberal ethical system with an emphasis on sexual exploration.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ra%C3%ABlism

item.title: St. Louis Light
item.description:
The St. Louis Light, also known as the St. Louis Ghost Light or Ghost Train, is a paranormal phenomenon reported near St. Louis, Saskatchewan, Canada. Witnesses describe it as a large white beam of light, similar to a locomotive’s headlamp, that moves along an abandoned rail line. This phenomenon has been featured on the television series Unsolved Mysteries and has inspired various folklore explanations, including a ghost train or the spirit of a brakeman. In 2014, Canada Post issued a stamp depicting the St. Louis ghost train as part of a series on Canadian ghost tales.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St._Louis_light

item.title: Chupacabra
item.description:
The chupacabra is a legendary creature in the folklore of the Americas, named for its alleged habit of attacking and drinking the blood of livestock. Descriptions of the creature vary, with some depicting it as reptilian and alien-like, while others describe it as more dog-like. Initial sightings occurred in Puerto Rico in 1995, and the creature has since been reported in various locations globally, though these reports are anecdotal and often attributed to animals like canids afflicted with mange. Investigations suggest that many sightings can be explained by known animals with diseases or by misidentification, with some descriptions potentially influenced by popular culture.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chupacabra

item.title: Space Brothers (conspiracy theory)
item.description:
“Space Brothers” is a UFO conspiracy theory that emerged in the 1950s, initially describing benevolent space travelers. Early portrayals often depicted these beings as “Aryan” or Nordic, but later media, like Close Encounters of the Third Kind, featured gray-skinned aliens. By the late 1980s, the concept of benevolent Space Brothers began to be overshadowed by narratives of malevolent Gray Aliens associated with abductions and mutilations. The term itself gained traction in the popular press by 1954, with figures like George Adamski and his followers claiming contact with friendly extraterrestrials.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Brothers_%28conspiracy_theory%29

item.title: 1952 Washington, D.C. UFO incident
item.description:
In July 1952, Washington D.C. experienced a series of unidentified flying object (UFO) sightings, known as the “Washington flap.” These sightings, particularly on July 18–19 and July 26–27, garnered significant media attention and were considered a peak event for UFO reports investigated by Project Blue Book. The Air Force, led by Major General John A. Samford, explained the visual sightings as misidentified phenomena like stars or meteors, and the radar contacts as being caused by temperature inversions. Despite the official explanation, some witnesses and researchers maintained their belief that the targets were solid, metallic objects. The widespread reports also prompted concern from the White House and led to the CIA forming a study group, which eventually contributed to the creation of the Robertson Panel to further investigate UFOs.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1952_Washington%2C_D.C._UFO_incident

item.title: Sightings
item.description:
Sightings is an American paranormal and news television show that began in the 1990s. Initially airing as a special titled The UFO Report: Sightings in 1991, it evolved into a weekly program investigating topics like UFOs, ghosts, and Bigfoot. The show was presented in an investigative news format and hosted by Tim White. Sightings aired on various networks, including Fox, and later in syndication, before concluding its run in 1998 with special episodes.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sightings_%28TV_series%29

item.title: List of ufologists
item.description:
This article lists notable individuals who investigate whether UFOs are linked to extraterrestrial aliens, a field known as ufology. The list includes figures from various backgrounds such as theorists, journalists, scientists, and authors. These individuals have contributed to the study and discussion of UFO phenomena through research, writing, and public discourse.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ufologists

item.title: George Adamski
item.description:
George Adamski was a Polish-American author known for his claims of contact with extraterrestrial beings, whom he called “Space Brothers.” He gained prominence in ufology circles in the 1940s and 1950s after displaying photographs he asserted were of alien spacecraft. Adamski authored several books detailing his alleged meetings with Nordic aliens and his purported flights with them to other planets. While his accounts were popular, many researchers considered him a charlatan, and his claims have been widely scrutinized and debunked.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Adamski_Foundation

item.title: Otis T. Carr and His Flying Saucer Claims
item.description:
Otis T. Carr emerged in the 1950s flying saucer scene, founding OTC Enterprises in 1955 with the stated aim of advancing technology inspired by Nikola Tesla. Carr patented a flying saucer design, the Ezechiel Wheel, and claimed to be developing a full-size version capable of lunar travel. He also invented other devices like “The Gravity Electric Generator” and “The Utron Electric battery.” Despite receiving significant publicity, including from Fate Magazine, Carr’s business ventures were considered fraudulent. He was convicted in 1961 for selling unregistered securities and served part of a jail term.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Otis_T._Carr

item.title: Truman Bethurum
item.description:
Truman Bethurum was a well-known UFO contactee from the 1950s, famous for his 1954 book Aboard a Flying Saucer. He claimed to have been contacted by aliens from a utopian planet called Clarion, located on the other side of the Moon. Bethurum also founded the Sanctuary of Thought, a communal organization. While some believed him to be sincere, many UFO researchers dismissed his claims as implausible.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanctuary_of_Thought

item.title: Unarius Academy of Science
item.description:
Unarius is a non-profit organization founded in Los Angeles, California, in 1954, which claims to advance an “interdimensional science of life” based on “fourth-dimensional” physics principles. The organization was founded by Ernest Norman and his wife Ruth Norman, who claimed to channel messages from advanced intelligent beings. Unarius centers exist in various countries, and the organization has published over 100 volumes of channeled material since its founding. The group’s beliefs include reincarnation, the existence of ancient interplanetary civilizations, and contact with “Space Brothers.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unarius_Academy_of_Science

item.title: The Black Vault Launches Searchable UFO Files Release Archive
item.description:
The Black Vault has launched a new searchable archive for the recently released UFO Files Release #1 collection. This archive, built on a custom search engine, makes the government’s UAP document release more accessible than the original format. The Black Vault processed the files to remove password restrictions and improve searchability, including generating searchable text from documents. The platform offers direct access to records, with features like searchable PDFs, videos, and metadata, and will expand as new UAP records are released.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/the-black-vault-launches-searchable-ufo-files-release-archive

item.title: Ufology
item.description:
Ufology is the investigation of unidentified flying objects (UFOs) by individuals who believe they may have extraordinary origins, often extraterrestrial. While governments and private groups have investigated UFOs, ufology is generally considered a pseudoscience by skeptics and science educators. The field has historical roots in phenomena like “mystery airships” and “foo fighters,” and has seen increased public interest, particularly in recent years. Despite its classification as pseudoscience, some academic projects and governmental bodies are now studying unidentified anomalous phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ufology

item.title: Mutual UFO Network
item.description:
The Mutual UFO Network (MUFON) is a US-based nonprofit organization of civilian volunteers dedicated to the scientific study of UFO sightings. Founded in 1969, it has grown to over 4,000 members worldwide and claims to have investigators in every U.S. state and over 43 countries. MUFON has faced criticism for its focus on pseudoscience and claims that its investigators do not always use the scientific method. The organization has also experienced internal controversy regarding the alleged far-right viewpoints of some officials and donors.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutual_UFO_Network

item.title: Public opinion on extraterrestrial life
item.description:
Public opinion surveys indicate a widespread belief in the existence of intelligent alien civilizations, often exceeding scientific confidence levels. Studies show significant cross-national variation in belief, with some research highlighting a gap between public perception and expert consensus. Demographic factors, such as religious observance and age, correlate with differing beliefs, and psychological factors like exposure to UFO content also play a role. Public interest in extraterrestrial life is further reflected in search trends and the popularity of media related to speculative theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_opinion_on_extraterrestrial_life

item.title: Close encounter
item.description:
In ufology, a “close encounter” is defined as witnessing an unidentified flying object (UFO) at a close range, reducing the likelihood of misidentification. This classification system was first proposed by J. Allen Hynek in his 1972 book, The UFO Experience: A Scientific Inquiry. Hynek’s original scale included six levels, ranging from nocturnal lights to encounters involving animated entities. Later, Jacques Vallée expanded this scale to include close encounters of the fourth and fifth kinds, with the latter referring to human-initiated contact with extraterrestrial life.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Close_encounter

item.title: Interdimensional UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The interdimensional UFO hypothesis (IUH) proposes that UFO sightings result from experiencing other coexisting dimensions. This idea has been advanced by ufologists such as Meade Layne, John Keel, J. Allen Hynek, and Jacques Vallée, who suggest UFOs are a modern manifestation of phenomena historically attributed to mythological or supernatural beings. Concepts similar to “other dimensions” appear in various religious and mystical traditions, including Sufism’s ‘ālam al-mithāl. In the 19th century, spiritualists and figures like H. P. Blavatsky popularized the idea of “planes” or “dimensions.” Meade Layne is considered an early proponent, claiming flying discs were “etheric” and could materialize from or dematerialize into another state.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdimensional_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Ghost rockets
item.description:
Ghost rockets were rocket or missile-shaped unidentified flying objects first sighted in 1946, primarily in Sweden and other Scandinavian countries. While many sightings were attributed to meteors, some displayed characteristics inconsistent with natural phenomena, such as reported maneuverability. Investigations considered origins from former German rocket facilities, with theories suggesting Soviet tests of captured German missiles. However, some military opinions, including a declassified USAF document, suggested the possibility of extraterrestrial origins for these and later flying saucer reports. Similar sightings were also reported in Greece, Portugal, Belgium, and Italy, with investigations in Greece being halted by the army after conferring with foreign officials.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_rockets

item.title: Pana Wave
item.description:
The Pana-Wave Laboratory is a division of the Japanese new religious group Chino-Shoho. This group claimed that scalar electromagnetic waves are harmful and that their members wore white robes and affixed spiral patterns to their vehicles for protection. The organization was known for its anti-communist ideology and its claims about environmental pollution from artificial scalar electromagnetic waves. The group gained temporary fame in 2003 due to its unusual activities, which led to media coverage and investigations by authorities.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pana_Wave

item.title: Ground Crew Project
item.description:
The Ground Crew Project is a UFO religion founded in the 1980s by Sheldan Nidle, which believes Earth is on the verge of a cosmic transformation. Adherents expected this transformation to occur as Earth passed through a “photon belt” in the 1990s, facilitated by contact with an extraterrestrial organization called the “Galactic Federation.” Members were prepared to act as intermediaries between humans and these extraterrestrials. Despite failed predictions of mass landings and transformations, the group continues to believe in impending contact and the need to spiritually prepare humanity.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ground_Crew_Project

item.title: Ashtar
item.description:
Ashtar, sometimes called Ashtar Sheran, is the name given to an extraterrestrial being or group of beings that some people claim to have channeled. George Van Tassel was the first to claim receiving an Ashtar message in 1952, and after his introduction, other mediums also claimed contact. These messages often included predictions, such as imminent landings of spaceships on Earth, which ultimately failed to materialize. Ashtar is often depicted as a humanoid with blond hair and European features, and the Ashtar movement is studied as a prominent form of UFO religion.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ashtar_Galactic_Command

item.title: Universe People
item.description:
Universe People, also known as Cosmic People of Light Powers or Heavenly Angels, is a Czech UFO religion and doomsday cult founded in the 1990s. Centered around Ivo A. Benda, the group believes extraterrestrial civilizations communicate with contactees. They are known for opposing RFID chipping, which they claim is an attempt by “Saurians” to enslave humanity. The movement has attracted media attention and is considered a distinctive UFO religion in the Czech Republic.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universe_People

item.title: UFO sightings in outer space
item.description:
Astronauts have reported sightings of unidentified flying objects (UFOs) in space, which ufologists claim as evidence of extraterrestrial life. However, many of these alleged sightings have been explained as man-made debris, rocket stages, or misidentified objects like fishing boats or antenna covers. NASA has stated that after years of manned space voyages, no evidence of extraterrestrial spacecraft has been found. Some reports, like those concerning Apollo 11, have been attributed to hoaxes or misinterpretations of astronaut statements.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_sightings_in_outer_space

item.title: All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office
item.description:
The All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) is a U.S. Department of Defense office established in July 2022 to investigate unidentified anomalous phenomena across air, sea, and space. It evolved from previous initiatives like the Unidentified Aerial Phenomena Task Force (UAPTF) and the Airborne Object Identification and Management Synchronization Group (AOIMSG). AARO’s mission includes standardizing data collection, analyzing sightings, and assessing potential threats to national security. A March 2024 report by AARO found no empirical evidence of extraterrestrial technology in historical U.S. government investigations.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/All-domain_Anomaly_Resolution_Office

item.title: 1947 flying disc craze
item.description:
In the summer of 1947, a widespread “flying disc craze” swept across the United States following civilian pilot Kenneth Arnold’s sighting of disc-shaped objects. This event, widely publicized by the media, led to hundreds, possibly thousands, of similar reports in the following weeks. Explanations ranged from novel technology and mass hysteria to extraterrestrial origins and occult interpretations. The craze has been studied as the “birth of a modern myth,” with scholars examining it within folklore and religious studies frameworks.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1947_flying_disc_craze

item.title: The Spacecraft Hypothesis: A Different Form of Life and Its Cultural Impact
item.description:
The space animal hypothesis suggests that flying saucers and UFOs could be living organisms from Earth’s atmosphere or interplanetary space, rather than alien spacecraft. This idea has been proposed by various authors since at least 1923, with John Philip Bessor being an early modern proponent in the late 1940s. Throughout the 1950s and beyond, numerous individuals and publications explored this concept, with some suggesting UFOs behaved like animals or were even “space critters.” The hypothesis has also been depicted in fiction, including Arthur Conan Doyle’s stories and Jordan Peele’s 2022 film Nope, where a UFO is revealed to be an animal.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_animal_hypothesis

item.title: The Age of Disclosure
item.description:
“The Age of Disclosure” is a 2025 American documentary film directed by Dan Farah that explores claims of alien intelligence on Earth and a government cover-up. The film features interviews with former U.S. government officials, military personnel, and intelligence community members who assert that alien life and technology have been concealed for decades. It premiered at SXSW on March 9, 2025, and was later released theatrically and on Amazon Prime Video on November 21, 2025. While it achieved significant VOD success, critics noted a lack of physical evidence and an over-reliance on eyewitness testimony, leading to mixed reviews.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Age_of_Disclosure

item.title: Time-traveler UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The time-traveler hypothesis proposes that unidentified flying objects are humans traveling from the future using advanced technology. This idea, also known as “chrononaut UFO” or “future humans,” has been publicly discussed by figures like former NASA engineer Larry Lemke and filmmaker Steven Spielberg. While considered extremely implausible by mainstream scholars, the concept has roots in early science fiction and has been explored in various media. The hypothesis suggests that these future humans might be visiting for reasons such as historical study or tourism.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time-traveler_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: John Philip Bessor
item.description:
John Philip Bessor was a correspondent and author for Fate Magazine, known for his 1947 space animal hypothesis. This theory proposed that “flying saucers” might be biological entities rather than technological spacecraft. Bessor’s writings on this and other topics, including hauntings and mysterious lights, appeared in various publications throughout the 1950s. His hypothesis influenced other writers and researchers in the field of UFO studies.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Philip_Bessor

item.title: Pentagon Releases Batch of Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena Files
item.description:
The Pentagon has released approximately 160 declassified documents concerning unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP), including never-before-seen footage and historical materials dating back to the 1940s. These files contain reports of various UAP sightings, such as “orbs launching orbs” and a “misshapen and uneven ball of white light,” as well as accounts from Apollo crews. The release aims to provide public transparency, though the government has not claimed to have detected extraterrestrial life. Public opinion suggests a belief that the government may be concealing information about UAP, with some thinking they are alien in origin.

Source: https://www.wired.com/story/pentagon-drops-new-ufo-files

item.title: Pentagon Releases Files Related to Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP)
item.description:
The Pentagon has begun releasing government files concerning unidentified anomalous phenomena (UAP), also known as UFOs. These files include FBI reports on unexplained sightings and NASA debriefs with astronauts who observed unidentified objects on the moon. Images released alongside the files depict various phenomena, including an object seen by a U.S. military operator over the Middle East in May 2022 and a football-shaped UAP reported near Japan in 2024. Archival photographs from the Apollo 12 and Apollo 17 missions also show unidentified phenomena on the lunar surface.

Source: https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/see-the-pentagons-new-ufo-image-release

item.title: UFO Sightings and Unidentified Aerial Phenomena: An Analysis of Historical Cases
item.description:
Black triangles are UFOs described as large, silent, dark, and triangular, often seen at night with pulsating lights. A UK Ministry of Defence report suggested these sightings could be attributed to “buoyant plasmas” or meteor entry, though its scientific validity is debated. Notable sightings include the 1997 Phoenix Lights, the 1989-1992 Belgian wave, and incidents in Hudson Valley, Southern Illinois, Tinley Park, and Stephenville, Texas. Some theories propose that classified military aircraft, such as the speculative Northrop TR-3A Black Manta, may be responsible for some black triangle reports.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_triangle_%28UFO%29

item.title: Archives of the Impossible
item.description:
The Archives of the Impossible (AOTI) is a special collection at Rice University in Houston, Texas, established in 2014. It houses research materials on paranormal and UFO subjects, including papers from Jacques Vallée and declassified Stargate Project documents. AOTI is recognized as the first publicly accessible archive with a primary focus on UFO and paranormal research. The collection has grown significantly, encompassing millions of documents and supporting research initiatives.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archives_of_the_Impossible

item.title: Nibiru cataclysm
item.description:
The Nibiru cataclysm is a predicted disastrous encounter between Earth and a large planetary object, often referred to as Nibiru or Planet X. This doomsday scenario was first proposed in 1995 by Nancy Lieder, who claimed to receive messages from extraterrestrials. The idea has since spread through various internet groups and has been linked to the 2012 phenomenon and other astronomical events. Astronomers and planetary scientists have widely rejected the Nibiru cataclysm as pseudoscience, stating there is no scientific evidence to support such claims.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nibiru_cataclysm

item.title: Travis Walton incident
item.description:
The Travis Walton incident details an alleged alien abduction of American forestry worker Travis Walton on November 5, 1975, in the Apache–Sitgreaves National Forests near Heber, Arizona. Walton claimed he was taken aboard a flying saucer after investigating an unusual light. After being missing for five days, Walton reappeared and sold his story to a tabloid, which was later adapted into the film Fire in the Sky. Skeptics have alleged the incident was a hoax, potentially to avoid penalties for a forestry contract. In 2021, a key witness, Mike Rogers, recanted his testimony, stating the event was staged, though he later reconciled with Walton and retracted his confession.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skyfire_Summit

item.title: Dan Aykroyd's Awards and Honors
item.description:
Daniel Edward Aykroyd is a Canadian-American actor, comedian, screenwriter, and producer, born on July 1, 1952. He gained fame as a writer and cast member of Saturday Night Live from 1975 to 1979, where he created iconic characters like the Coneheads and the Blues Brothers. Aykroyd is widely recognized for his roles in films such as The Blues Brothers, Ghostbusters, and Trading Places. Beyond acting, he is also a businessman, co-founding the House of Blues music venues and the Crystal Head Vodka brand.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dan_Aykroyd

item.title: Black helicopter
item.description:
The “black helicopter” is a symbol associated with conspiracy theories, particularly concerning a potential military takeover of the United States within the American militia movement. These theories, which emerged in the 1970s and were linked to cattle mutilation reports, have also been connected to UFOs and “men in black.” While some politicians and groups have promoted these ideas, various government agencies and defense contractors do operate black or unmarked helicopters for unclassified purposes. The concept has also been used metaphorically to dismiss or ridicule other conspiracy theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_helicopter

item.title: Walter Siegmeister
item.description:
Walter Isidor Siegmeister, also known by pseudonyms like Raymond W. Bernard, was an American alternative health advocate and esoteric writer. He is recognized for merging the Hollow Earth theory with religious beliefs about UFOs. Siegmeister founded several utopian communes and authored books on health and nutrition, though his ideas were largely dismissed as pseudoscience. He died in 1965.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_Siegmeister

item.title: Jim Marrs
item.description:
Jim Marrs was an American journalist and author known for his work on alleged cover-ups and conspiracies. His 1989 book, Crossfire, which explored JFK assassination conspiracy theories, was a source for Oliver Stone’s film JFK. Marrs also wrote books addressing government conspiracies related to aliens, 9/11, and secret societies. He taught a class on the JFK assassination at the University of Texas at Arlington for 30 years and passed away in 2017 at the age of 73.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jim_Marrs

item.title: Men in black
item.description:
In popular culture and UFO conspiracy theories, “men in black” (MIB) are described as government agents in dark suits who harass UFO witnesses. These figures are typically depicted as tall, pale men with expressionless faces, wearing black suits and sunglasses. Stories of men in black have inspired various media, including the Men in Black film franchise and songs by bands like The Stranglers. Ufologists have incorporated MIB into their theories, with some accounts dating back to the 1940s and 1950s.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Men_in_black

item.title: Donald Keyhoe
item.description:
Donald Edward Keyhoe was a Marine Corps naval aviator and writer who became a UFO researcher in the 1950s. He advocated for the U.S. government to research and publicly release its UFO files. Keyhoe authored several influential books on the topic, including “The Flying Saucers Are Real,” which argued that UFOs were extraterrestrial and that the government was suppressing information. He also co-founded the National Investigations Committee On Aerial Phenomena (NICAP) to further UFO research and advocacy.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Keyhoe

item.title: Glenn Dennis
item.description:
Glenn Dennis, born March 24, 1925, and died April 28, 2015, was a founder of the International UFO Museum and Research Center in Roswell, New Mexico. He began working at Ballard Funeral Home in Roswell at age 15 and later became responsible for the funeral home’s military contract with Roswell Army Air Field. Dennis gained attention from UFO researchers in 1994 after contacting a tips hotline following an episode of Unsolved Mysteries about the Roswell UFO incident. While his accounts were featured in books about the incident, serious doubts were raised about his testimony, with creators of a documentary discovering he provided a fictitious name for a nurse who allegedly gave him classified information.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glenn_Dennis

item.title: Aetherius Society
item.description:
The Aetherius Society is a new religious movement founded by George King in the mid-1950s, based on his claimed contacts with extraterrestrial intelligences. Its followers aim to cooperate with these “Cosmic Masters” to solve Earth’s problems and advance into a New Age. The religion is described as syncretic, incorporating elements of theosophy, millenarianism, New Age beliefs, and UFO religion. Key practices include altruism, community service, nature worship, spiritual healing, and physical exercise.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aetherius_Society

item.title: Agent Steel
item.description:
Agent Steel is an American speed metal band formed in Los Angeles in 1984. Known for vocalist John Cyriis’s high-pitched vocals and interest in UFOs, the band has reformed multiple times since their initial disbandment in 1988. They have released several studio albums throughout their career, with their most recent being “No Other Godz Before Me” in 2021. Original drummer Chuck Profus passed away in June 2025.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agent_Steel

item.title: When Prophecy Fails
item.description:
“When Prophecy Fails” is a 1956 social psychology book by Leon Festinger, Henry Riecken, and Stanley Schachter. The book details their study of a UFO religion in Chicago called the Seekers, who believed the world would be destroyed by a flood on December 21, 1954. The authors investigated the members’ coping mechanisms and cognitive dissonance when the prophecy failed to materialize. The study proposed five conditions that could lead believers to increase their commitment after a prophecy disconfirmation. Later archival work has challenged the book’s core claims, alleging fabrications and exaggerations by the authors.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/When_Prophecy_Fails

item.title: NASA Documents Show Renewed Internal Planning on How to Announce Discovery of Extraterrestrial Life
item.description:
NASA has released documents detailing internal discussions about how the agency would communicate a confirmed discovery of extraterrestrial life. These documents include a June 2025 meeting invitation focused on developing a formal communications protocol. This planning builds upon earlier work, with a 2020 presentation outlining foundational considerations for such a scenario, emphasizing equitable information dissemination and managing public reaction. While the documents confirm ongoing planning, they do not indicate any discovery has occurred, and significant portions of the deliberative process remain redacted.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/nasa-documents-show-renewed-internal-planning-on-how-to-announce-discovery-of-extraterrestrial-life

item.title: U.S. Department of War Forms 'Space Tiger Team' to Address Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena in Space
item.description:
A Department of War document, obtained via FOIA, reveals the 2023 formation of a “UAP Space Tiger Team” led by the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO). This initiative aims to develop a framework for addressing unidentified anomalous phenomena in space, including spaceborne and transmedium objects. The effort is tied to legislative requirements and involves multiple commands and agencies, such as U.S. Space Command and NORAD. The team’s work is intended to normalize UAP considerations within defense planning and close domain-awareness gaps.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/newly-released-documents-show-uap-space-tiger-team-built-around-space-and-transmedium-cases

item.title: UFOs in fiction
item.description:
Numerous works of fiction, including films, television shows, video games, and literature, have featured UFOs and alien invasions. These narratives often depict hostile alien forces attacking Earth, with humanity fighting back. Examples range from early science fiction like “The War of the Worlds” to modern films such as “Independence Day” and video games like “Destroy All Humans!”. Television series also explore these themes, categorizing UFO depictions into in-universe realities, hoaxes, or misidentified terrestrial craft.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFOs_in_fiction

item.title: Moths at Attracted by Floodlight and the 'Rod' Phenomenon
item.description:
In cryptozoology and ufology, “rods” are elongated visual artifacts seen in photographs and videos. While some proponents claim they are paranormal or alien in nature, optical analyses suggest they are insects captured over multiple wing beats, creating an illusion of a single, rod-like body. Investigations, including a documentary by China Central Television, have demonstrated that these phenomena are often caused by motion blur from slow camera exposure times and the way flying insects are recorded. José Escamilla was a notable proponent who claimed to have filmed rods, interpreting them as alien life forms.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rod_%28optical_phenomenon%29

item.title: Jean Miguères and the Raelian Movement
item.description:
Jean-Richard Miguères was a French UFO contactee and ufologist who claimed to have been visited by extraterrestrials after a car accident in 1969. He founded the UFO organization CEIRUS, which was deemed a cult by the anti-cult group ADFI. Miguères was killed by his father-in-law, a member of ADFI, who believed him to be a cult leader. He was also a friend of Raël, the creator of Raëlism.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CEIRUS

item.title: Space jellyfish
item.description:
A “space jellyfish” is a phenomenon caused by sunlight reflecting off high-altitude rocket plume gases during morning or evening twilight. This luminous apparition, resembling a jellyfish, has led to public panic and reports of unidentified flying objects. The article lists numerous rocket launches, primarily Falcon 9 missions, that have produced this visual effect. These sightings have occurred at various locations, including Vandenberg Space Force Base and Florida.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_jellyfish

item.title: Mystery airship
item.description:
The mystery airship phenomenon involved thousands of people across the United States reporting sightings of unidentified flying lights and airborne craft from late 1896 through mid-1897. These reports are considered a precursor to modern UFO sightings. While some accounts described humanoid occupants, often claiming to be from Mars, the prevailing belief was that these airships were the creation of an unknown inventor. Skeptics argue that the technology of the era could not have produced such craft, suggesting that many reports were likely hoaxes or misidentifications, especially given the prevalence of “yellow journalism” at the time.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mystery_airship

item.title: Majestic 12
item.description:
Majestic 12, also known as Majic-12 or MJ-12, is a purported organization that emerged in fake documents circulated by ufologists in 1984. These documents claim it was a secret committee formed in 1947 by President Harry S. Truman to investigate alien spacecraft. The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) has declared these documents “completely bogus,” and many consider them a hoax. Despite this, the concept remains popular in UFO conspiracy theories and has appeared in various forms of popular culture.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Majestic_12

item.title: Skinwalker Ranch
item.description:
Skinwalker Ranch, previously known as Sherman Ranch, is a property in Utah reputed to be a site of paranormal and UFO-related activities. Claims about the ranch first appeared in 1996, detailing alleged inexplicable and frightening events experienced by a family who occupied the property. These claims include sightings of unidentified flying objects, vanishing cattle, and unusual animal encounters.

The ranch was purchased by Robert Bigelow in 1996 to study these phenomena, and later by Brandon Fugal in 2016. The property has been the subject of investigations and media portrayals, including a television series on the History channel. Skeptics have questioned the validity of the claims, suggesting they may be illusory or fabricated.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skinwalker_Ranch

item.title: List of reported UFO sightings
item.description:
This article is a dynamic list of reported unidentified flying object (UFO) sightings, some of which include claims of close encounters or alien abductions. While most UFOs are eventually identified as known objects or phenomena, a small number remain unexplained. UFO sightings are reported frequently, with notable historical waves occurring in 1947 in the United States and ongoing reports from various countries. The article also lists common shapes reported in UFO sightings, such as orbs, triangles, and discs, and notes that the term “unidentified anomalous phenomena” (UAP) is sometimes used to avoid cultural associations with UFO conspiracy theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_reported_UFO_sightings

item.title: The Vril Myth and Its Influence on Right-Wing Esotericism in Germany
item.description:
The Vril Society is a fictitious secret society that conspiracy theories claim existed in Germany in the early to mid-twentieth century. These theories suggest it was involved in the rise of Nazism and used supernatural energies to develop flying machines. While historical evidence for such a society is lacking, the concept of “Vril” originated from Edward Bulwer-Lytton’s 1871 novel The Coming Race, where it represented a vital energy. The legend of the Vril Society gained prominence through pseudohistorical texts and conspiracy theories that emerged after World War II, linking it to Nazi occultism and advanced technology.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vril_Society

item.title: New Emails Reveal Pentagon Effort to Align Messaging on AATIP and Luis Elizondo
item.description:
Newly released Pentagon emails from May 2019 reveal an internal contradiction regarding Luis Elizondo’s role in the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP). A senior Pentagon official, Neill Tipton, stated in an email that Elizondo had no assigned responsibilities related to AATIP during his time under Tipton. However, other emails show Tipton was directly communicating with Elizondo about AATIP and reviewing a memo concerning responsibilities for it. Pentagon officials expressed concern about these contradictions and the need for consistent messaging. Despite Tipton’s 2019 statement, previously released records show him engaging in direct discussions with Elizondo about AATIP responsibilities in 2017. Luis Elizondo disputes Tipton’s characterization, stating that documentary evidence shows Tipton was aware of his involvement and agreed to assume his responsibilities.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/new-emails-reveal-pentagon-effort-to-align-messaging-on-aatip-and-luis-elizondo

item.title: Valley of the Dawn
item.description:
The Valley of the Dawn, or Vale do Amanhecer, is a new religious movement and UFO religion founded in Brazil in the 1960s. It was established in 1969 by Tia Neiva, a medium who received guidance from a spirit named Pai Seta Branca. The movement incorporates elements of Christianity, Spiritism, Umbanda, UFO beliefs, and esoteric traditions. Its main temple is located in Planaltina, Federal District, Brazil, and the community includes residents, directors, and mediums who perform daily spirit-healing rituals.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Valley_of_the_Dawn

item.title: The Flying Saucer Conspiracy
item.description:
The Flying Saucer Conspiracy is a 1955 book by Donald Keyhoe that alleges a United States government conspiracy to conceal information about flying saucers. Keyhoe claimed the existence of a “silence group” orchestrating this cover-up, which he believed was motivated by a desire to prevent public hysteria. The book also discussed alleged UFO sightings, including the disappearance of an F-89 fighter over Lake Superior and the famous Flight 19 incident. Keyhoe’s theories influenced later UFO conspiracy narratives, though historian Felix Harcourt notes Keyhoe’s less nefarious view of the “silence group” compared to subsequent theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Flying_Saucer_Conspiracy

item.title: The Flying Saucers Are Real
item.description:
Donald Keyhoe’s 1950 book, The Flying Saucers Are Real, investigated United States Air Force reports of UFOs between 1947 and 1950. The book, which followed a similar article published in True magazine, popularized many ideas in ufology. Keyhoe argued that the Air Force had a policy of concealing UFO existence until 1949, after which it shifted to gradual revelation. He also contended that Earth had been visited by extraterrestrials for two centuries, with increased activity after the 1945 atomic bomb test.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Flying_Saucers_Are_Real

item.title: William L. Moore and His Contributions to UFOlogy
item.description:
William L. Moore, born in 1943, is an author and former UFO researcher known for his work from the late 1970s to the late 1980s. He co-authored books with Charles Berlitz, including “The Philadelphia Experiment - Project Invisibility” and “The Roswell Incident,” which alleged extraterrestrial involvement in the Roswell incident. Moore also circulated the Majestic 12 documents, which purported to reveal a secret government group overseeing UFOs. Critics have described his books as unsubstantiated and his work on the Majestic 12 documents as fraudulent.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Moore_%28ufologist%29

item.title: Fourth International–Posadist
item.description:
The Fourth International–Posadist is a Trotskyist organization founded in 1962 by J. Posadas. Posadism, a strain of communism developed by the group, incorporates ufology, arguing that visiting aliens must come from advanced communist societies and will aid in world revolution. The movement initially had followers in Cuba, Bolivia, and Brazil, but faced conflict with mainstream left-wing groups and government crackdowns in Cuba. Interest in Posadism, particularly its ufological aspects, resurged in the 2010s through online communities.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourth_International%E2%80%93Posadist

item.title: Morris Ketchum Jessup
item.description:
Morris Ketchum Jessup was an American ufologist with a Master of Science degree in astronomy. He is best known for his writings on UFOs, including his 1955 book The Case for the UFO. Jessup’s work explored theories linking ancient monuments to prehistoric superscience and speculated about the nature of UFOs. His death in 1959 was ruled a suicide, but circumstances surrounding it have fueled conspiracy theories, particularly concerning the Philadelphia Experiment.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_K._Jessup

item.title: Gloria Lee
item.description:
Gloria Lee was an American airline flight attendant and a prominent figure in the 1960s contactee movement. She founded the Cosmon Research Foundation and claimed to receive telepathic communications from a Venusian entity named J. W. Lee authored two books based on these alleged communications. She died at the age of 36 following a hunger strike in Washington, D.C., after politicians did not take her claims seriously.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmon_Research_Foundation

item.title: Kosmopoisk
item.description:
Kosmopoisk, also known as Spacesearch, is a pseudoscientific organization founded in 1980 that investigates ufology, cryptozoology, and other mysteries. The group expanded into an international movement in 2001 and officially registered as the All-Russian Scientific Organization in 2004. Kosmopoisk organizes expeditions to various locations to research anomalous phenomena and creatures. The organization claims to have over 2,500 members in numerous groups across 25 countries and has conducted over 250 expeditions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kosmopoisk

item.title: Newly Released Pentagon Records Center on UAP and Internal Email Communications
item.description:
A collection of Department of Defense records has been released, focusing on internal email communications involving Pentagon spokesperson Christopher Sherwood and references to Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (UAP). These documents stem from a Freedom of Information Act request seeking insight into how UAP-related discussions occurred within the Pentagon’s public affairs office. The request specifically targeted emails sent to or from Sherwood containing keywords like “UAP.” While 416 pages were found responsive, portions were withheld under FOIA exemptions related to intelligence information and internal deliberations. The Black Vault has filed an appeal regarding the extensive redactions.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/pentagon-spokesperson-christopher-sherwood-emails-on-ufos-uap-and-more

item.title: Siderella
item.description:
Siderella, also known as Iso-Zen, was a French UFO religion founded in 1966 by Jean-Paul Appel. The group combined ufological and New Age themes with elements of Buddhism and Tantrism, believing in an imminent apocalypse where aliens would save its members and transport them to Alpha Centauri. Members lived communally, practiced consciousness expansion, and engaged in group sex as a form of “tantric transmutation.” Siderella was often described as a cult by media outlets and was officially classified as a “UFO cult” in France.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siderella

item.title: The Unexplained Files
item.description:
The Unexplained Files is an American documentary television series that aired on Science Channel. The series investigated paranormal and unexplained phenomena across two seasons, with the first season having 8 episodes and the second season planning 12. While it was the most watched series on the Science Channel during its first season, critics noted its tendency to focus on sensationalized theories over scientific explanations. One review highlighted how a veterinarian’s explanation for cattle mutilations was given minimal airtime compared to “outlandish theories,” leading to criticism of the show as biased.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Unexplained_Files

item.title: Secret Space Program and 20 and Back: Fictional Stories as Fact
item.description:
The “secret space program” and “20 and back” are fictional narratives presented as fact within ufology. These stories describe individuals recruited into secret military programs for service on other planets, followed by age regression or time travel to return to their original time. The origins of these stories are linked to a 1970s British spoof documentary called “Alternative 3,” which evolved into a widespread myth. Corey Goode popularized these concepts, particularly on the Gaia program “Cosmic Disclosures,” claiming he was age-regressed after serving as a warrior with alien species. The terms “Secret Space Program” and “20 and Back” are trademarks owned by Goode.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secret_space_program

item.title: Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program
item.description:
The Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) was a U.S. government study focused on unidentified flying objects (UFOs) and unexplained aerial phenomena. Established in 2007 and funded with $22 million over five years, it began within the Defense Intelligence Agency. The program officially ended in 2012, though investigations into UFOs continued through successor programs. AATIP generated a 494-page report documenting alleged UFO sightings and funded 38 studies on advanced aerospace topics.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Advanced_Aerospace_Threat_Identification_Program

item.title: The Extraterrestrial UFO Hypothesis (ETH)
item.description:
The extraterrestrial hypothesis (ETH) proposes that some unidentified flying objects (UFOs) are physical spacecraft piloted by intelligent extraterrestrial beings. While historical speculation about alien visitors dates back centuries, the modern ETH gained traction in the late 1940s and 1950s, influenced by early science fiction and widespread UFO sightings. Despite some historical government and private studies suggesting extraterrestrial origins, the scientific community largely attributes UFO reports to misinterpretations, hoaxes, or common phenomena, with no definitive physical evidence of alien visitation found.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extraterrestrial_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Gray Barker
item.description:
Gray Barker was an American writer known for his books on UFOs and paranormal phenomena, including his 1956 book They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers. This book introduced the concept of the Men in Black to ufology. Despite writing about these subjects, Barker was reportedly skeptical of paranormal claims and sometimes wrote about them for financial gain. He also occasionally participated in hoaxes to mislead UFO investigators.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gray_Barker

item.title: Endangered Species (1982 film)
item.description:
Endangered Species is a 1982 American science fiction horror film directed by Alan Rudolph. The story follows a former New York City police officer who relocates to rural Colorado and becomes entangled in an investigation of cattle mutilations. These events lead to theories involving UFOs and cults, but ultimately point to secret government experiments. The film stars Robert Urich and JoBeth Williams, with a plot inspired by reported cattle mutilations.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endangered_Species_%281982_film%29

item.title: Aligned, Multiple-transient Events in the First Palomar Sky Survey
item.description:
Two companion papers, “Aligned, Multiple-transient Events in the First Palomar Sky Survey” and “Transients in the Palomar Observatory Sky Survey (POSS-I) may be associated with nuclear testing and reports of unidentified anomalous phenomena,” were published in October 2025. These papers, authored by Beatriz Villarroel and Stephen Bruehl, suggest that observed anomalies in 1950s Palomar Observatory sky survey images might be linked to nuclear testing and UFO reports. The research’s conclusions have been met with significant skepticism from the scientific community, with critics proposing alternative explanations for the observed phenomena. ArXiv declined to archive preprints of the papers, citing a lack of sufficient scholarly research.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aligned%2C_Multiple-transient_Events_in_the_First_Palomar_Sky_Survey

item.title: Condon Committee
item.description:
The Condon Committee, officially the University of Colorado UFO Project, was funded by the United States Air Force from 1966 to 1968 to study unidentified flying objects. Directed by physicist Edward Condon, the project examined numerous UFO files and reported sightings, concluding that further study was unlikely to yield significant scientific discoveries. The committee’s final report, known as the Condon Report, was published in 1968 and has been cited as a factor in the reduced academic interest in UFOs. The report’s conclusions received a mixed reception, with some critics arguing it was biased and that a significant number of cases remained unexplained.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condon_Committee

item.title: Reports from Project Grudge and Project Blue Book
item.description:
Project Grudge was a U.S. Air Force project that investigated UFOs from February to December 1949. Its stated goal was to alleviate public anxiety by explaining UFO sightings as conventional phenomena, mass hysteria, hoaxes, or psychopathological individuals. The project’s sole formal report concluded there was no evidence of foreign development and recommended reducing the scope of UFO investigations. Critics like J. Allen Hynek and Edward J. Ruppelt argued Project Grudge was more of a public relations campaign than an objective scientific examination.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Project_Grudge

item.title: John Lear (aviator)
item.description:
John Olsen Lear (December 3, 1942 – March 29, 2022) was an American aviator and UFO conspiracy theorist, known for his claims of alien collusion with secret governmental forces. He was the son of Learjet magnate Bill Lear and flew cargo planes for the CIA during the Vietnam era. In the 1980s, Lear became a prominent figure in UFO conspiracy theories, notably introducing journalist George Knapp to Bob Lazar and his stories about Area 51. Lear’s statements influenced the UFO movement, contributing to theories about government cover-ups and alien interactions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Lear

item.title: Foo fighter
item.description:
During World War II, Allied aircraft pilots used the term “foo fighters” to describe unidentified flying objects or mysterious aerial phenomena observed in the European and Pacific theaters. These sightings, formally reported from November 1944, were initially believed to be enemy secret weapons. Explanations considered by the Robertson Panel included electrostatic phenomena, electromagnetic phenomena, or light reflections. The term “foo” originated from cartoonist Bill Holman’s “Smokey Stover” comic strips in the early 1930s.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foo_fighter

item.title: Alien abduction
item.description:
Alien abduction is the phenomenon where individuals claim to have been kidnapped by extraterrestrial beings for physical and/or psychological experimentation. While most scientists attribute these experiences to factors like suggestibility, sleep paralysis, or psychopathology, some researchers and individuals believe they are real. Typical claims involve medical examinations, warnings about environmental issues or nuclear weapons, and sometimes interspecies breeding. Reports of alien abductions are most common in English-speaking countries, with the Betty and Barney Hill case in 1961 being one of the first widely publicized instances.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_abduction

item.title: International UFO Museum and Research Center
item.description:
The International UFO Museum and Research Center is located in Roswell, New Mexico, and was established in 1991 as a nonprofit educational organization. It is largely focused on the 1947 Roswell incident and other UFO events. The museum features exhibits on UFO encounters and an extensive library of related materials. It also serves as a central point for the annual UFOfest held in Roswell.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_UFO_Museum_and_Research_Center

item.title: Stanton T. Friedman
item.description:
Stanton Terry Friedman was an American-Canadian nuclear physicist and ufologist known for his research into the Roswell UFO incident. He dedicated his career to investigating unidentified flying objects, giving lectures and publishing numerous papers on the subject. Friedman supported the hypothesis that the Roswell incident involved an extraterrestrial spacecraft crash and believed UFO sightings were consistent with magnetohydrodynamic propulsion. He also famously debated the existence of extraterrestrial life and its presence on Earth with Seth Shostak.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanton_T._Friedman

item.title: Psychosocial UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The psychosocial hypothesis (PSH) in ufology suggests that some UFO reports are best explained by psychological or social factors, rather than extraterrestrial origins. This perspective, popular in the UK and France, contrasts with the extraterrestrial hypothesis (ETH). PSH researchers view UFOs as a subject worthy of study, even with a skeptical approach, and propose that many sightings stem from misperceived mundane objects influenced by an unusual emotional climate. The hypothesis also considers that claims of direct alien contact can be explained by psychological processes, given the known uninhabitability of planets like Venus and Mars. Furthermore, research has explored parallels between UFO narratives and science fiction, suggesting that cultural influences and historical contexts shape these accounts.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychosocial_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: GEIPAN: Study and Information Group on Unidentified Aerospace Phenomena
item.description:
GEIPAN, the Unidentified Aerospace Phenomena Research and Information Group, is a unit of the French space agency CNES. Founded in 1977 as GEPAN, it has undergone several name changes and is dedicated to investigating unidentified aerospace phenomena and making its findings public.
