The organization’s mission includes drawing on data from reports, including those channeled by the French Gendarmerie, and utilizing CNES’s technical resources for scientific investigation. GEIPAN began making its archives available online in March 2007, and recent reports estimate that around 3% of sightings remain unidentified.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEIPAN

item.title: Hoaxes Related to Unidentified Flying Objects
item.description:
Numerous hoaxes related to the study of unidentified flying objects have been perpetrated throughout history. These include practical jokes like a balloon with a burning basket in 1897 and a turkey vulture with a burning cotton ball, which led to a school fire. Other hoaxes involved fabricated accounts of airships, such as one where a cow was reportedly lifted by a ship, and the controversial “alien autopsy” film footage that was later revealed to be a hoax. Photographic hoaxes, like those of George Adamski and a purported alien at Ilkley Moor, have also been exposed.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_UFO-related_hoaxes

item.title: Solway Firth Spaceman: The Enigmatic Figure in Jim Templeton's Photograph
item.description:
The Solway Firth Spaceman refers to a figure in a photograph taken on May 23, 1964, by Jim Templeton. Templeton claimed the figure, appearing in a spacesuit, was not visible when he took the picture. Journalist David Clarke suggested the figure was Templeton’s wife, whose blue dress was overexposed to appear white. Despite Templeton’s insistence on the photo’s authenticity, analysts confirmed it was genuine, and the figure has been widely debated by ufologists and paranormal investigators.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solway_Firth_Spaceman

item.title: The Seekers: A UFO Religion in Mid-Twentieth Century United States
item.description:
The Seekers, also known as The Brotherhood of the Seven Rays, was a UFO religion founded in 1953 by Charles Laughead in the Midwestern United States. Led by Dorothy Martin, the group believed flying saucers would save them from a predicted catastrophe on December 21, 1954. When the prophecy failed, many members experienced cognitive dissonance, a phenomenon studied by Leon Festinger in his book When Prophecy Fails. The Seekers are considered by some to be the earliest UFO religion.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Seekers_%28rapturists%29

item.title: The Urantia Book
item.description:
The Urantia Book is a spiritual, philosophical, and religious text published in 1955, claiming to be composed by celestial beings. It presents a cosmology that includes the planet Earth as “Urantia” and aims to unify religion, science, and philosophy. The book covers topics such as the origin and meaning of life, humanity’s place in the universe, Earth’s history, and the life of Jesus. Its copyright expired in the United States in 1983, making it a public domain work.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Urantia_Book

item.title: Snippy the Mutilated Horse: A 50-Year Mystery
item.description:
The mutilation of “Snippy” the horse, first widely reported on October 5, 1967, involved the death and alleged dissection of a Colorado horse. Mainstream experts attributed the death to natural causes, but sensationalized press and folklore linked it to Satanic cults, intelligence operations, or flying saucers. The case was the first to associate extraterrestrial beings and UFOs with animal mutilations. The horse’s skeletal remains are now part of a UFO tourism attraction.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutilation_of_%22Snippy%22_the_horse

item.title: Nazi UFOs
item.description:
Claims and stories circulate in ufology, conspiracy theories, and science fiction that link UFOs to Nazi Germany. These theories suggest that Nazi Germany developed advanced aircraft or spacecraft before and during World War II, and that these craft, along with their creators, survived post-war in secret bases. During World War II, unusual aerial sightings were often attributed to novel Nazi technology, and in the early Cold War, Western nations speculated that these sightings might be due to Soviet use of captured or reverse-engineered Nazi technology. Investigations into phenomena like “foo fighters” and “ghost rockets” sometimes led to suspicions of advanced German aircraft or weapons.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nazi_UFOs

item.title: Megatron: The UFO-Shaped Restaurant
item.description:
The Megatron was a flying saucer-designed building in Cambridgeshire, UK, constructed in 1990 as a restaurant. It was conceived by local businessman Danny Blundell and designed by Graham Campbell, featuring innovations like touchscreen ordering. Upon opening, locals mistook it for a UFO, prompting police investigation. The restaurant closed in 1992 due to high costs and was later taken over by McDonald’s, before ultimately being demolished in 2008.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Megatron_%28building%29

item.title: Mark-Age
item.description:
Mark-Age is an American UFO religion founded in 1962 in Miami, Florida, by Pauline Sharpe and Charles Boyd Gentzel. The group incorporates New Age, UFO contactee, and Theosophical elements, believing itself to be a contact point with extraterrestrials who govern the Solar System. Members believe in channeling messages through telepathy and automatic writing, and they were affiliated with the contactee Gloria Lee. The organization’s headquarters later moved to Pioneer, Tennessee.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mark-Age

item.title: Reptilian conspiracy theory
item.description:
Reptilians, also known by various other names, are reptilian humanoids featured in fantasy, science fiction, ufology, and conspiracy theories. Conspiracy theorist David Icke popularized the idea that shapeshifting reptilian aliens control Earth by assuming human form and gaining political power. The concept of reptilians has roots in early 20th-century fiction and occult writings. These theories have appeared in various political contexts and have been linked to other conspiracy theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reptilian_conspiracy_theory

item.title: Abu Fanous: The Mysterious Light Phenomenon in the Arabian Desert
item.description:
Abu Fanous is a mysterious light phenomenon observed in the Arabian desert, often appearing at dawn or night as a moving orb that lures travelers into remote areas before vanishing. In Arabic folklore, it is described as a jinn that causes people to become lost and die, with advice to recite religious verses to ward it off. While theories suggest natural gases igniting, no clear scientific explanation exists for Abu Fanous. The phenomenon has inspired art and video games, and there are accounts of travelers encountering it.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abu_Fanous

item.title: Puerto Rico Highway 303
item.description:
Puerto Rico Highway 303 is a rural road connecting Cabo Rojo and Lajas. A section of this highway, spanning the southern border of Sierra Bermeja, is known as the “Puerto Rico Alien Route” due to numerous UFO reports in the area. While a sign designating it as the “Ruta Extraterrestre” was installed, it was removed after the Puerto Rico Department of Transportation and Public Works rejected official recognition. The municipality of Lajas and local businesses now promote this route as a tourist attraction.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puerto_Rico_Highway_303

item.title: Milton William Cooper
item.description:
Milton William “Bill” Cooper was an American conspiracy theorist, radio broadcaster, and author known for his 1991 book Behold a Pale Horse. In this book, he discussed global conspiracies involving extraterrestrial life and claimed HIV/AIDS was a man-made disease. Cooper was killed in 2001 by sheriff’s deputies after shooting at them during an attempted arrest. His work has been influential in UFO and militia circles and has been referenced in popular culture.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milton_William_Cooper

item.title: Archive of Luis Elizondo’s ‘Deleted’ Emails
item.description:
Luis Elizondo is a central figure in discussions about Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (UAP) due to his alleged involvement with the Pentagon’s Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP). The Black Vault reported that the Department of Defense (DoD) destroyed Elizondo’s emails, hindering verification of his claims. To circumvent this, The Black Vault pursued FOIA requests for emails of individuals who likely corresponded with Elizondo, indirectly recovering some exchanges. This archive contains these recovered emails, as well as records where “no records” were found, offering insight into Elizondo’s interactions within the DoD.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/archive-of-luis-elizondos-deleted-emails

item.title: Alien Abduction and Reptilian Hypothesis
item.description:
This article lists alleged extraterrestrial beings reported in close encounters or speculated to be associated with UFOs. It details descriptions of beings such as the Flatwoods monster, Greys, Hopkinsville goblins, Nordic aliens, and Reptilians. The article also includes cryptozoological animals and mentions “rods” or “skyfish” as elongated visual artifacts. Explanations for these phenomena range from extraterrestrial or interdimensional origins to skepticism, cultural tracking, or hoaxes.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_alleged_extraterrestrial_beings

item.title: List of reported UFO sightings in the United States
item.description:
This article is a list of alleged UFO sightings in the United States, detailing various incidents with dates, locations, and brief descriptions. Notable events include the 1897 Aurora, Texas airship crash, the 1942 “Battle of Los Angeles,” the 1947 Roswell incident, and the 1961 Betty and Barney Hill abduction. The list spans from 1865 to 2023, documenting numerous reported encounters with unidentified flying objects.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_reported_UFO_sightings_in_the_United_States

item.title: Chir batti: A Ghost Light Phenomenon in Banni Grasslands, Gujarat
item.description:
Chir batti, also known as chhir batti or cheer batti, is a ghost light reported in the Banni grasslands and adjoining desert of the Rann of Kutch in Gujarat, India. Local villagers call it “chir batti,” meaning ghost light, and describe it as an unexplained, bright, and colorful light that moves like a ball of fire. These lights have been a part of local folklore for centuries and are said to appear on dark nights, sometimes misleading travelers. Modern science generally attributes such phenomena to the oxidation of phosphine, diphosphane, and methane produced by organic decay.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chir_batti

item.title: Caso ET de Varginha: Uma Análise Completa
item.description:
The Varginha UFO incident in 1996 involved claims of sightings of strange creatures and a UFO in Varginha, Brazil, which received significant media attention. Associated claims included the capture of extraterrestrial beings by authorities and unexplained animal deaths at a zoo. An investigation by the Brazilian Army concluded that a mentally unstable individual was mistaken for an alien and military movements were routine. The incident has significantly impacted tourism in Varginha, with the city incorporating UFO imagery into its branding and attractions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Varginha_UFO_incident

item.title: Area 51: The Dreamland Chronicles
item.description:
Area 51: The Dreamland Chronicles is a 1997 non-fiction book by David Darlington about Area 51 in Nevada. Reviews noted the book’s service in detailing the site’s early history while also criticizing its inclusion of accounts from “alien seekers” and “saucer nuts.” The book has been called a definitive work on Area 51 and its cultural impact, with one review describing it as even-handed towards ufologists. It was published by Henry Holt & Company.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Area_51:_The_Dreamland_Chronicles

item.title: The Flying Saucer (song)
item.description:
“The Flying Saucer,” released in July 1956 by Buchanan and Goodman, is a novelty song that pioneered the mashup genre. It interweaves segments of popular songs with spoken news commentary to narrate a fictional alien invasion. The song reached number 3 on the Billboard Hot 100 and faced lawsuits for its use of sampled material. Ultimately, it was legally recognized as a new work, paving the way for future sampling practices in music.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Flying_Saucer_%28song%29

item.title: Rachel, Nevada
item.description:
Rachel, Nevada, is a census-designated place with a population of 48 as of the 2020 census. It is known for being the closest settlement to Nellis Air Force Range and Area 51, attracting aviation enthusiasts and UFO hunters. The town features an alien-themed establishment called the Little A’Le’Inn, which serves as a motel, restaurant, and bar. Rachel was named after Rachel Jones, the first baby born in the valley.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rachel%2C_Nevada

item.title: Billy Meier
item.description:
Eduard Albert Meier, known as Billy, is the founder of a UFO religion and claims to be a contactee who has communicated with extraterrestrials called Plejaren. He presented photographs, metal samples, sound recordings, and film footage as evidence of alien spacecraft. Skeptics and ufologists widely characterize Meier’s claims as fraudulent, suggesting he used models to hoax his photographs. His ex-wife stated that photos of alleged extraterrestrial women were actually of members of the singing and dancing troupe The Golddiggers.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Freie_Interessengemeinschaft_f%C3%BCr_Grenz-_und_Geisteswissenschaften_und_Ufologiestudien

item.title: UFO Watchtower
item.description:
The UFO Watchtower, located north of Hooper, Colorado, is an observation platform and campground that opened in May 2000. It was established by cattle farmer Judy Messoline, who converted her barn into a tourist attraction to capitalize on reported UFO sightings in the San Luis Valley. The attraction relies on donations and sales of alien-themed merchandise for funding. Over 30,000 visitors have reportedly visited the Watchtower since its opening, with reported sightings and a “healing garden” also being features.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Watchtower

item.title: Thomas Allen LeVesque
item.description:
Thomas Allen LeVesque (1948–2018) was an American conspiracy theorist known for promoting legends of the Hollow Earth, The Shaver Mystery, and Dulce Base. He authored “The Dulce Base” under the pseudonym Jason Bishop III, detailing claims of a pact between humans and aliens and underground bases. According to author Adam Gorightly, LeVesque later confessed that these stories were fabricated as creative writing.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Allen_LeVesque

item.title: Nevada State Route 375
item.description:
State Route 375 in Nevada, officially named the Extraterrestrial Highway, is a 98.4-mile route that passes through remote desert terrain near the Nellis Air Force Range. The highway gained notoriety due to its proximity to Area 51 and numerous reported UFO sightings, leading to its renaming in 1996 to attract tourism. The small town of Rachel, located along the route, caters to visitors interested in extraterrestrial themes with themed businesses. Despite its unique designation and the allure of alleged alien activity, SR 375 remains a lightly traveled road.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nevada_State_Route_375

item.title: FAA Records Detail Pilot Encounter with Unidentified Object Pacing Aircraft Over Nevada
item.description:
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) records obtained through a FOIA request detail an unusual aerial encounter over Northern Nevada in May 2025 involving a business jet. Air traffic control audio and internal FAA logs document an object that was visually observed, appeared aircraft-like, and maintained a parallel course with the jet, but was not detected on radar. The object was described with evolving terminology, shifting from an undefined “black” object to something resembling a “fighter” before rapidly ascending and disappearing. The FAA withheld radar data, citing Sensitive Security Information, and the encounter occurred in an area near multiple military testing facilities.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/faa-records-detail-pilot-encounter-with-unidentified-object-pacing-aircraft-over-nevada

item.title: FOIA Request on UAP Keywords Produces Minimal Results, Heavy Redactions, from Energy Department
item.description:
A Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request to the U.S. Department of Energy for communications regarding unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP) yielded only two partially released documents. These documents, internal DOE emails from May 2024, were prepared for Secretary Jennifer Granholm in advance of an oversight hearing. The emails reference attached briefing materials that included “UFO and UPA talking points” for congressional engagement. Despite the request’s broad scope, significant portions of the released documents were withheld under FOIA exemptions, preventing the disclosure of substantive content or internal analysis on UAP topics.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/foia-request-on-uap-keywords-produces-minimal-results-heavy-redactions-from-energy-department

item.title: Ley line
item.description:
Ley lines are straight alignments drawn between historic structures, prehistoric sites, and landmarks, a concept developed in early 20th-century Europe. Proponents believed ancient societies erected structures along these lines, and later, some in the Earth Mysteries movement suggested they demarcate “earth energies” and serve as guides for alien spacecraft. However, archaeologists and scientists consider ley lines to be an example of pseudoarchaeology and pseudoscience, noting that alignments can occur by chance and that the idea of earth energies lacks scientific verification.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ley_line

item.title: Project Sign report
item.description:
Project Sign, also known as Project Saucer, was a U.S. Air Force study of unidentified flying objects active for most of 1948. Established to collect and evaluate UFO sighting information due to potential national security concerns, it was the precursor to Project Grudge. A public paper released in 1949 stated that while some UFOs appeared to be actual aircraft, their origins could not be determined due to insufficient data. The project investigated claims related to inventor Jonathan Edward Caldwell’s disk-rotor aircraft, but found they could not explain the widespread “flying saucer” reports.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Project_Sign

item.title: Ancient Astronaut Theory
item.description:
The “ancient astronauts” theory, also known as paleocontact, proposes that intelligent extraterrestrial beings visited Earth in ancient times and influenced human civilization. Proponents suggest these beings were responsible for advancements in technology, religion, and culture, and that deities from various religions are extraterrestrial in origin. However, academics and archaeologists largely dismiss these claims as pseudoarchaeology and unscientific, noting that evidence presented is often distorted or fabricated. Some scholars also argue that ancient astronaut theories can carry racist undertones by diminishing the accomplishments of indigenous cultures.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_astronauts

item.title: Haim Eshed
item.description:
Haim Eshed is a retired brigadier general in Israeli Military Intelligence and is considered the father of Israel’s space program, having been responsible for launching 20 Israeli satellites. In 2020, Eshed gained notoriety for promoting a UFO conspiracy theory. He claimed that world governments have been secretly collaborating with extraterrestrials, including a “Galactic Federation,” and that there is a joint base on Mars. These claims were made in an interview and subsequently in a book, though some experts, including the current chairman of the Israel Space Agency, have expressed skepticism about his unconventional interpretations of space signals.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haim_Eshed

item.title: Linda Moulton Howe
item.description:
Linda Moulton Howe is an American investigative journalist and documentary filmmaker known for her work in ufology and her investigations into cattle mutilations, which she attributes to extraterrestrials. She also speculates about government involvement with aliens. Howe has produced documentaries on environmental issues and UFO-related phenomena, earning a Regional Emmy award for her 1980 documentary “A Strange Harvest.” She has been a frequent guest on various media outlets discussing her theories on unexplained phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linda_Moulton_Howe

item.title: Christ Brotherhood
item.description:
The Christ Brotherhood, Inc. was a UFO religious group founded on December 16, 1956, in Orange, California, by Wallace C. Halsey. Halsey claimed to channel extraterrestrial messages warning of world destruction and the salvation of a remnant of humanity. The group began constructing a compound with underground bomb shelters in Cache Valley, Utah, in 1961, intended to house about 1000 people. The organization lost cohesion after Halsey disappeared in a plane crash in March 1963, though his teachings were later published posthumously.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christ_Brotherhood

item.title: The Epsilon Team Conspiracy Theory in Greece
item.description:
The Epsilon Team is an alleged secret society featured in modern Greek folklore, conspiracy theories, and ufology. First described in a 1977 book, it is believed to consist of prominent Greeks possessing secret knowledge of extraterrestrial origin. Over time, literature surrounding the Epsilon Team incorporated anti-Semitic conspiracy theories, portraying a cosmic battle between the Epsilon Team and Jewish people. This body of beliefs is known as “epsilonism,” and its adherents are called “epsilonists.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epsilon_Team

item.title: AATIP Resurfaces in 2022 TS-SCI Briefing, According to Navy FOIA Release
item.description:
Two Freedom of Information Act requests to the Department of the Navy yielded a single responsive record: an email chain detailing a March 2022 briefing on the Advance Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) and the Airborne Object Identification and Management Synchronization Group (AOIMSG). This briefing, held at Roosevelt Hall on Fort McNair, included classified TS-SCI sessions and discussed topics such as congressional reports and “Tic-Tac” incidents. Despite redactions, the record’s responsiveness to requests specifically seeking information involving Brennan P. McKernan and Luis Elizondo suggests their names were present in the communications. The reappearance of AATIP in a 2022 briefing, alongside its successor AOIMSG, complicates previous characterizations of the program’s scope and Elizondo’s role.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/aatip-resurfaces-in-2022-ts-sci-briefing-according-to-navy-foia-release

item.title: UFO reports and disinformation
item.description:
Governments have historically used censorship, counter-intelligence, cover-ups, and disinformation to protect national security secrets, including those related to secret aerial objects. In 1947, the Army claimed recovered debris near Roswell was from a weather balloon, but later Air Force historians concluded this was likely a cover for Project Mogul. By the late 1970s, an organized campaign to spread disinformation about UFOs was in operation, and since the 1950s, the U.S. Air Force has systematically spread false UFO stories. Numerous individuals, including authors, journalists, and military personnel, have claimed that government or military insiders were spreading stories about extraterrestrial spacecraft.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_reports_and_disinformation

item.title: Center for UFO Studies (CUFOS)
item.description:
The Center for UFO Studies (CUFOS) is a privately funded UFO research group founded in 1973 by J. Allen Hynek. Hynek, who had previously consulted for the U.S. Air Force’s Project Blue Book, established CUFOS to continue the scientific study of UFO reports after the project’s closure. The organization maintains a library and archives of UFO-related materials and compiles and analyzes UFO incident reports from around the world. CUFOS is now based in Chicago, Illinois, and its case files were moved to Albuquerque, New Mexico, in 2020.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Center_for_UFO_Studies

item.title: Despite Trump’s Call to Release UAP Files, Navy Denies Appeal for 78 Classified UAP Photographs
item.description:
The U.S. Navy has formally denied an appeal seeking the release of 78 photographs designated as “unidentified aerial phenomena” (UAP). This decision upholds a previous denial of a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request filed by The Black Vault. The Navy cited classification under Executive Order 13526 and the UAP Classification Guide as the reason for withholding the images. Despite public calls for transparency and a former president’s stated desire to release such files, the Navy’s appeal authority deferred to the Original Classification Authority, stating that no portion of the photographs could be released.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/despite-trumps-call-to-release-uap-files-navy-denies-appeal-for-78-classified-uap-photographs

item.title: National UFO Conference
item.description:
The National UFO Conference (NUFOC) was an annual conference based in San Diego, California, with a mission to present research in ufology. It began in 1964 as the Congress of Scientific Ufologists and was the longest active conference in the United States for presenting information on the UFO phenomenon. In 2004, James W. Moseley retired as Permanent Chairman, and Lisa Davis took over as Executive Director, aiming to increase public awareness of UFO phenomena by expanding contacts within the entertainment community. Davis, active in ufology since 1997, also founded The Foundation For Abductee Research and Support.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_UFO_Conference

item.title: Rauni-Leena Luukanen-Kilde
item.description:
Rauni-Leena Luukanen-Kilde was a Finnish physician who became known for her writings and lectures on parapsychology, ufology, and mind control. After a car accident led to her retirement from medicine, she turned her attention to the paranormal and organized the first international conference on extraterrestrials in Finland. Luukanen-Kilde claimed to have been rescued by extraterrestrials and alleged a suppressed human-alien exchange program. She also spoke about secret agencies using mind control technology and a plot involving the swine flu vaccine.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rauni-Leena_Luukanen-Kilde

item.title: UFO
item.description:
The 2018 American science fiction film UFO stars Gillian Anderson and Alex Sharp. It follows a mathematics student who attempts to prove the existence of extraterrestrials after a UFO sighting causes electromagnetic interference. With the help of his professor, he deciphers a message containing the fine-structure constant and coordinates. The film was released directly to video on September 4, 2018.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_%282018_film%29

item.title: Rational Culture
item.description:
Rational Culture is a Brazilian UFO religion that originated from Umbanda and was founded in Rio de Janeiro in 1935. It is based on a series of books called Universo em Desencanto, which cover topics such as cosmology, metaphysics, and UFOs. The movement emphasizes sequential reading of these books as the path to “salvation” or “Rational Immunization.” Rational Culture has a complex cosmology with four planes of existence and recognizes seven main religions, viewing their founders as beings from the lower astral.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rational_Culture

item.title: 1967 Malmstrom Air Force Base UFO Incident
item.description:
In 1996, retired Air Force personnel claimed a 1967 weapons failure at a Montana nuclear missile complex was linked to a UFO sighting over Malmstrom Air Force Base, an event known as the Malmstrom UFO incident. Skeptics suggest the UFO was likely the planet Mars and unrelated to the malfunction, and a military investigation found no connection to any UFO sighting. However, a 2025 Pentagon report revealed the incident was caused by a classified test of an electromagnetic pulse device.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malmstrom_UFO_incident

item.title: UFO Coverup? Live
item.description:
UFO Coverup? Live was a US television broadcast that aired on October 14, 1988, and introduced the Majestic 12 hoax and Area 51 to Americans. Hosted by Mike Farrell, the program discussed various UFO incidents and featured interviews with ufologists, pilots, and skeptics. It also included clips from films with alien themes and presented claims from informants about aliens and a government agreement for an Area 51 base. The program later faced criticism for its truthfulness and was noted for influencing The X-Files.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Cover_Up%3F_Live

item.title: Ängelholm UFO memorial
item.description:
The Ängelholm UFO memorial in Sweden commemorates an alleged UFO landing site. It features a concrete model of a flying saucer and landing impressions, based on the account of Gösta Carlsson. Carlsson claimed a UFO encounter in 1946 led to his business success, though investigations have not corroborated his story. Despite the lack of evidence, the monument, erected in 1972, has become a tourist attraction and is maintained by the local government.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%84ngelholm_UFO_memorial

item.title: UFO: The True Story of Flying Saucers
item.description:
UFO (full title: Unidentified Flying Objects: The True Story of Flying Saucers) is a 1956 American semi-documentary film exploring the UFO phenomenon in the United States. The film begins by recounting early publicized UFO sightings and the US Air Force’s initial investigations, including Project Sign. It then follows Albert M. Chop, a public information officer, as his views on UFOs evolve from skepticism to belief in their unknown, possibly extraterrestrial, origin. The documentary analyzes notable UFO footage and culminates with a recreation of the 1952 Washington, D.C. UFO incident, in which Chop played a significant role.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_%281956_film%29

item.title: Albert K. Bender
item.description:
Albert K. Bender was an author and ufologist, born on June 16, 1921, and died on March 29, 2016. He served in the United States Army Air Forces during World War II and founded the International Flying Saucer Bureau in 1952. Bender claimed to have had supernatural encounters, including a telepathic visitation from three “men in black” in 1953, which he later recounted in his 1962 book, Flying Saucers and the Three Men. In 1965, he also established the Max Steiner Music Society.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_K._Bender

item.title: They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers
item.description:
They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers is a 1956 book by Gray Barker that alleged “Men in Black” were involved in covering up the existence of flying saucers. The book details Barker’s involvement with Albert K. Bender and the International Flying Saucer Bureau, and claims Bender and other investigators were silenced by men in black suits. Barker’s work, which also recounts the Maury Island incident, became a bestseller and contributed to UFO folklore. The book’s “Men in Black” concept influenced later popular culture, including The X-Files and Men in Black films.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/They_Knew_Too_Much_About_Flying_Saucers

item.title: 2019–20 Colorado drone sightings
item.description:
Between December 2019 and January 2020, numerous unidentified drones were sighted in northeastern Colorado and western Nebraska, flying in grid formations at night. Investigations by the FAA, FBI, and local law enforcement did not determine the operator of these drones. While some reports suggested possible explanations including military exercises or misidentified aircraft, the Colorado Department of Public Safety’s investigation concluded that most sightings were attributable to planets, stars, commercial aircraft, and hobbyist drones. The sightings prompted concern from local residents and officials, including Colorado’s governor.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Colorado_drone_sightings

item.title: Former President Obama Clarifies on Aliens During Podcast Interview
item.description:
Former President Barack Obama clarified his stance on extraterrestrials in an interview released on Saturday. While initially joking about aliens in a podcast clip, he later posted the clip to Instagram with a caption to provide further explanation. Obama stated that while statistically, life likely exists elsewhere in the vast universe, the chances of alien visitation are low. He emphasized that he saw no evidence of extraterrestrial contact during his presidency.

Source: https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/barack-obama-clarifies-seen-no-evidence-aliens-saying-real-rcna259232

item.title: Xenoarchaeology
item.description:
Xenoarchaeology is a hypothetical field of study concerned with the material remains of extraterrestrial cultures, primarily existing in science fiction. It is a branch of xenology that aims to reconstruct and interpret past alien civilizations. The field is not currently practiced by mainstream archaeologists due to a lack of actual material to study. Xenoarchaeology is also referred to as astroarchaeology or exoarchaeology and encompasses related concepts like SETI and SETA.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xenoarchaeology

item.title: Ratanabá conspiracy theory
item.description:
Ratanabá is a conspiracy theory about a lost city in the Brazilian Amazon rainforest, allegedly built by extraterrestrial beings. The theory was initiated by Urandir Fernandes de Oliveira and spread through social media, becoming a viral meme. Despite being debunked by archaeologists and dismissed as pseudoscientific, the theory gained traction, even being promoted by former Culture Secretary Mário Frias. The spread of misinformation related to Ratanabá has also threatened historical heritage sites, leading to official repudiation.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ratanab%C3%A1

item.title: International UFO Congress
item.description:
The International UFO Congress is an annual conference established in 1991 in Arizona that focuses on ufology. It moved to the Phoenix, Arizona area in 2011 after previously being held in Laughlin, Nevada. The event features presentations on topics like alien abduction, UFO sightings, and paranormal experiences, with speakers from around the world. The conference has changed ownership multiple times since its inception.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_UFO_Congress

item.title: World UFO Day
item.description:
World UFO Day is an awareness day observed annually by some on June 24 and others on July 2. The June date commemorates the first widely reported UFO sighting in the United States, while the July date marks the Roswell incident. The July 2 observance, declared by the World UFO Day Organisation, aims to raise awareness of UFOs and encourage governments to declassify related files. Celebrations involve sky-watching and discussions about the possibility of extraterrestrial life.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_UFO_Day

item.title: Marfa lights
item.description:
The Marfa lights are an optical phenomenon observed near Marfa, Texas, often seen from a publicized viewing area. Scientific investigations between 2000 and 2008 concluded that these lights are primarily caused by automobile headlights distorted by warm desert air. Historical accounts of the lights date back to 1883, and they have been featured in various media and inspired artistic works.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marfa_lights

item.title: SOBEPS: Belgian Society for the Study of Space Phenomena
item.description:
SOBEPS, the Belgian society for the study of space phenomena, was formed in 1971 and gained fame for investigating the 1989-1990 Belgian UFO wave. The group published a magazine called Inforespace and saw its membership fluctuate significantly over the years. Following the wave of sightings, SOBEPS collaborated with the Belgian Air Force. The society dissolved in 2007 due to financial difficulties, with COBEPS formed to continue some of its activities.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOBEPS

item.title: UFO Investigators League
item.description:
The UFO Investigators League (UFOIL) was a UFO group founded by Timothy Green Beckley in the early 1970s, which later re-emerged in 1990 in New Brunswick, New Jersey. Another chapter, founded in 1981 in Covington, Kentucky, investigated UFOs, ghosts, and Bigfoot sightings before disbanding in 1982. The Ohio UFO Investigators League (OUFOIL) operated a UFO hotline in 1973 from Fairfield, Ohio, and investigated various cases including the Loveland Lizard and Mothman. OUFOIL also hosted the 1978 MUFON Symposium and published a newsletter called “The Ohio Skywatcher.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Investigators_League

item.title: The Black Knight Satellite Mystery
item.description:
The Black Knight satellite conspiracy theory posits the existence of an extraterrestrial spacecraft in Earth’s polar orbit, with NASA allegedly concealing its origins. This theory is a compilation of various unrelated stories and interpretations. A 1998 NASA photo, claimed by some to depict the Black Knight, is identified by space journalist James Oberg as space debris, specifically a thermal blanket lost during a mission. Explanations for the legend’s origins include misinterpreted radio signals from Nikola Tesla’s experiments and long delayed radio echoes.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Knight_satellite_conspiracy_theory

item.title: Pentagon Refuses to Search for ‘Immaculate Constellation’ Emails
item.description:
The Department of War has refused to conduct a Freedom of Information Act search for emails related to the “Immaculate Constellation” program. This refusal is based on the department’s assertion that the program does not exist, therefore no responsive records could exist. This stance is unusual, as FOIA typically requires agencies to search for records regardless of the perceived validity of the subject matter. The phrase “Immaculate Constellation” entered public discourse in late 2024, leading to congressional attention and an official denial from the Department of Defense. Other agencies, like the NSA, have responded to similar requests with Glomar responses, acknowledging the request without confirming or denying the existence of records.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/pentagon-refuses-to-search-for-immaculate-constellation-emails

item.title: Istanbul UFO Museum
item.description:
The Istanbul UFO Museum, established in 2001 in Beyoğlu, Istanbul, Turkey, was the fourth international UFO museum. Founded by Haktan Akdoğan’s Sirius UFO Space Sciences Research Center, it housed photos, videos, reports, UFO-alien models, and a library on UFOs and extraterrestrial life. The museum reportedly had a mobile version that traveled around Istanbul in a truck in 2011. The Istanbul UFO Museum is no longer open.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Istanbul_UFO_Museum

item.title: Closed-Door 2024 UAP Briefing: FOIA Discloses Select Media Invitees
item.description:
Newly released records obtained through a Freedom of Information Act request reveal journalists who were privately invited to an embargoed Pentagon media roundtable on Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (UAP). This event, held on March 6, 2024, was connected to the Department of Defense’s first volume of the congressionally mandated Historical Record Report on U.S. government UAP programs. The invitation, sent by Pentagon spokesperson Susan Gough, outlined strict conditions for participation, including agreeing to an embargo until the report’s public release on March 8. Journalists from outlets such as The New York Times, CNN, Politico, Task & Purpose, and The Washington Post were among those invited, though some recipients remain redacted due to privacy concerns.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/closed-door-2024-uap-briefing-foia-discloses-select-media-invitees

item.title: Fiat Lux
item.description:
Fiat Lux is a neo-revelationist UFO religion founded in 1980 by Erika Bertschinger-Eicke, who was known as Uriella. Members of the group, primarily located in Germany’s Black Forest, believe in the imminent apocalypse and the arrival of alien ships to evacuate adherents to a new paradise called “Amora.” Uriella, who claimed to receive divine messages, died in 2019. The organization has been described as a cult and has an estimated following of thousands.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fiat_Lux_%28UFO_religion%29

item.title: New Lands
item.description:
New Lands is the second nonfiction book by Charles Fort, published in 1923 by Boni & Liveright. The book primarily addresses astronomical anomalies and is described as a satirical critique of astronomers. Fort criticizes astronomical predictions and scientific dogma, expanding on his theories of a “Super-Sargasso Sea” and continents above Earth. Despite its content, New Lands is considered Fort’s worst-regarded book, with its speculations on sky continents and Earth’s shape being seen as dated. The book had poor sales, leading to Boni & Liveright declining to publish Fort’s subsequent work.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Lands

item.title: Yankee Blue: Part of Pentagon UFO Disinformation Campaign
item.description:
Yankee Blue was a fake United States Air Force program that purported to involve the reverse-engineering of alien spacecraft. In reality, it was an internal hazing and misinformation campaign, possibly part of a broader Pentagon disinformation program. For decades, personnel in classified Air Force units were shown “UFO” photos and told they were assisting in a top-secret alien-technology recovery program. This ritual persisted from the Cold War era until its discontinuation in 2023. The strategy likely originated as a security measure to protect sensitive programs and divert attention from advanced weapons development.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yankee_Blue

item.title: Cultural Tracking in UFO Reports
item.description:
In ufology, cultural tracking describes how UFO reports evolve to mirror contemporary cultural changes and technological advancements. Historically, reported UFOs have reflected the era’s technology, from mysterious airships in the 1890s to flying saucers resembling current aerospace designs. This phenomenon also influences UFO narratives by incorporating elements from prevalent science fiction, such as car engine stoppages or specific alien appearances. Cultural tracking challenges the extraterrestrial hypothesis, suggesting instead a psychosocial explanation where UFOs and their occupants are imaginary, though some proponents of the interdimensional hypothesis believe advanced beings deliberately alter their appearance.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cultural_tracking

item.title: Psychological perspectives on UFO claims
item.description:
Claims of UFO sightings and contact have been the subject of extensive medical and psychological research, with some studies indicating potential psychological origins for these claims. Cognitive psychology explores how perception, biases, and reasoning shape beliefs in UFO phenomena, with concepts like pareidolia and apophenia being relevant. Psychiatric research has examined “experiencers” and found associations with conditions like paraphrenia and schizotypy, while some studies suggest UFO beliefs can be linked to a need for meaning. Personality traits, such as a fantasy-prone personality, have also been explored as a factor in UFO experiences.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_perspectives_on_UFO_claims

item.title: DoD IG Releases Final UAP Whistleblower Reprisal Report
item.description:
A heavily redacted Department of Defense Office of Inspector General (DoD OIG) release from January 7, 2026, details a UAP-related whistleblower reprisal investigation. This investigation, closed in February 2025, centered on the revocation of classified access for a complainant who had made protected disclosures about UAP matters to the DoD OIG. While the complainant’s name is redacted, the chronology and subject matter strongly overlap with publicly documented filings associated with David Grusch. The DoD OIG concluded that the protected disclosures were not a contributing factor to the revocation, finding that adverse actions were justified by substantiated findings of misconduct.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/dod-ig-releases-final-uap-whistleblower-reprisal-report

item.title: McMenamins Hotel Oregon UFO Festival
item.description:
The McMenamins UFO Festival is an annual event held in McMinnville, Oregon, that began in 2000. It was established to commemorate the 50th anniversary of the McMinnville UFO photographs. The festival is sponsored by McMenamins and has featured notable figures in the UFO community. By 2021, it attracted up to 10,000 attendees annually, making it the largest UFO enthusiast event in Oregon and the second-largest in the United States.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McMenamins_Hotel_Oregon_UFO_Festival

item.title: Longdendale lights
item.description:
The Longdendale lights are mystery lights observed in the Longdendale valley and surrounding areas of the Peak District in northern England, with reports dating back centuries and also known as the ‘Devil’s bonfires’. These lights can appear as a single bright light or small clusters of varying colors, sometimes moving rapidly, and have been seen by individuals and groups. Despite the proximity of a busy road, there is no physical evidence like photographs or video of the claimed lights, and explanations have included misidentification of distant lights, aircraft, or celestial bodies. Local legends attribute the lights to witches, spirits, or Roman soldiers’ ghosts, while some suggest misidentification of car or house lights, aircraft, Venus, or gamekeepers’ flashlights.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Longdendale_lights

item.title: Archives for the Unexplained
item.description:
Archives for the Unexplained (AFU), formerly Archives for UFO research, is a nonprofit foundation in Norrköping, Sweden, dedicated to collecting materials on UFOs, ufology, Forteana, cryptozoology, paranormal phenomena, and folklore. Founded in 1973 as the Work Group for Ufology, it was converted to a charitable foundation in 1980 and moved to its current location. AFU manages approximately 3.5 kilometers of shelf space containing vast collections of books, magazines, newspaper clippings, photographs, and reports, including Scandinavian and British archives. The organization also collects various related items like scale models, paintings, and memorabilia.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archives_for_the_Unexplained

item.title: Betz mystery sphere
item.description:
The Betz mystery sphere, an approximately 8-inch metal sphere, was discovered by the Betz family in Florida in 1974. The sphere reportedly reacted to sound, rolled on its own, and changed direction, leading Terry Betz to conduct experiments with it. At the time of discovery, the United States Navy suggested it was a ball bearing from a chemical plant’s piping system, and the Miami Herald reported a similar object in Jacksonville was identified as part of a paper mill valve. Later analysis by Skeptoid suggested the sphere was a ball check valve produced by Bell & Howell and proposed that uneven floors could explain its apparent autonomous movement.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betz_mystery_sphere

item.title: List of high-altitude object events in 2023
item.description:
In February 2023, multiple high-altitude airspace security events were reported across North America, Latin America, China, and Eastern Europe. Following the detection and downing of a Chinese balloon, the U.S. enhanced its radar monitoring, leading to increased detection of aerial objects. Several unidentified objects were shot down over North America, with investigations suggesting some may have been related to commercial or research entities. Recovery operations for some of these objects were suspended due to weather and low chances of retrieval, though debris from one object was later found to be from a weather monitoring equipment company.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_high-altitude_object_events_in_2023

item.title: COMETA Report
item.description:
The COMETA report is an unofficial 1999 publication by the French association COMETA concerning unidentified aerospace phenomena (UAP). Titled “UFOs and Defense: What Should We Prepare For?”, the report compiled UAP/UFO incidents over approximately 60 years, focusing on national defense implications. Contributors included defense experts and former officials from the French Air Force and space agency. The report concluded that a significant percentage of well-documented cases could not be explained by known sources, suggesting the extraterrestrial hypothesis as the most probable explanation for certain phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COMETA_report

item.title: Paul Bennewitz
item.description:
Paul Bennewitz was an American businessman and UFO investigator who believed he intercepted alien communications and discovered a secret underground facility called Dulce Base. His claims gained public attention in the early 1980s, and some sources suggest he was a target of a government disinformation campaign that led to his psychiatric hospitalization. Bennewitz’s investigation into cattle mutilations, which he linked to aliens, is also mentioned as a potential reason for government targeting. He died in 2003, and his story has been further publicized in books and documentaries.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paul_Bennewitz

item.title: Six and a Half Years Later, the DoD’s Reply to Harry Reid’s AATIP Memo Remains Missing
item.description:
In June 2009, Senator Harry Reid requested heightened protection for the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) in a letter to the Department of Defense. Despite the Department of Defense acknowledging the authenticity of this letter and stating it had responded, a formal response has not been publicly released. This absence of a documented reply conflicts with Pentagon statements and established procedures for handling congressional correspondence. The ongoing dispute over the existence and nature of AATIP, coupled with the difficulty in locating related documents, contributes to the mystery surrounding the program.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/six-and-a-half-years-later-the-dods-reply-to-harry-reids-aatip-memo-remains-missing

item.title: Kielce Bus Station
item.description:
The Kielce Bus Station in Poland is a novelty architecture building designed to resemble a UFO. Opened in 1984, it was considered modern at the time and became a landmark. The station features a circular main building within a large roundabout, with pedestrian access via tunnels to ensure safety. Despite its architectural significance, the building faced neglect in the 1990s and early 2000s, leading to a major reconstruction project that began in 2018.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kielce_Bus_Station

item.title: Integratron
item.description:
The Integratron is a cupola structure in Landers, California, designed by ufologist George Van Tassel, who claimed it could rejuvenate, provide anti-gravity, and enable time travel. Van Tassel stated he received instructions for its construction from Venusians and began building it in 1957, with the structure erected in 1959. After Van Tassel’s death, the building was acquired by sisters who now offer “sound baths” within the structure, promoting it as an acoustically perfect sound chamber. The Integratron was added to the National Register of Historic Places in April 2018.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integratron

item.title: UFO flap
item.description:
A UFO flap is defined as a surge in reports of unidentified airborne objects, often occurring in a specific geographical area. The term, originating from military language for chaos, is used by ufologists to describe these periods of increased sightings. These flaps can encompass various unrelated phenomena, and public interest, sightings, and news coverage of UFOs fluctuate in frequency. Hoaxers can sometimes exacerbate these periods of heightened interest.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_flap

item.title: Pentagon Reopens Search for Alleged ‘Yankee Blue’ Memo After Initial Denial
item.description:
A FOIA appellate decision has ordered the Department of War to conduct additional searches for a 2023 memorandum. This memorandum was reportedly issued by the Secretary of Defense’s office to halt an Air Force hazing ritual called “Yankee Blue,” which involved fabricated claims of reverse-engineering non-human technology. Initially, the Department of War stated no such memorandum was found, but the appeal has now been granted, requiring a new search. The original Wall Street Journal article described the alleged directive but did not publish the memo or provide specific sourcing.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/pentagon-reopens-search-for-alleged-yankee-blue-memo-after-initial-denial

item.title: Civilian Saucer Intelligence
item.description:
Civilian Saucer Intelligence (CSI) was an independent UFO research group founded in New York City in 1954. Unlike many amateur groups, CSI conducted rigorous investigations and published a well-regarded quarterly newsletter. The organization was critical of contactees but distinct in its investigation of close encounters involving alleged animate beings. CSI’s core personnel included Ted Bloecher, Isabel Davis, and Alexander Mebane, and the group was instrumental in translating French ufologist Aimé Michel’s books into English. CSI was defunct by 1959, though Davis and Bloecher remained active in UFO research.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civilian_Saucer_Intelligence

item.title: Flying Saucers from Outer Space
item.description:
Flying Saucers from Outer Space is a non-fiction book about UFOs written by Donald Keyhoe and published on October 5, 1953. The book was followed by The Flying Saucer Conspiracy in 1955 and preceded by The Flying Saucers Are Real in 1950. In 1956, a science-fiction film titled Earth vs. the Flying Saucers was made, suggested by Keyhoe’s book.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flying_Saucers_from_Outer_Space

item.title: The Report on Unidentified Flying Objects
item.description:
The Report on Unidentified Flying Objects is a 1956 book by Edward J. Ruppelt, who led Air Force UFO investigations. The book details his experiences and discusses various UFO sightings and official Air Force projects like Project Blue Book. Ruppelt’s work noted that while the Air Force officially dismissed the spaceship theory, internal conclusions were not unanimous. A later edition of the book saw Ruppelt declare his certainty that UFOs do not exist.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Report_on_Unidentified_Flying_Objects

item.title: List of UFO religions
item.description:
UFO religions are religious groups that center on alleged communication between humans and extraterrestrial beings. Adherents often believe that the arrival of alien civilizations will help humanity solve ecological, spiritual, and social problems. These belief systems are described as millenarian and can stem from beliefs in alien abduction. UFO religions have roots in Theosophy and share similarities with New Age thought, with the 1947 Roswell incident being a significant event in UFO spirituality.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_UFO_religions

item.title: National Institute for Discovery Science (NIDSci)
item.description:
The National Institute for Discovery Science (NIDSci) was a privately financed research organization that operated from 1995 to 2004 in Las Vegas, Nevada. Founded by Robert Bigelow, its purpose was to research fringe science and paranormal topics, particularly ufology, though it also investigated anomalies beyond just UFOs. NIDSci conducted research into areas like cattle mutilation and black triangle reports, and it purchased Skinwalker Ranch. The organization established a hotline in 1999 to receive reports of unusual occurrences, receiving over 5,000 submissions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Institute_for_Discovery_Science

item.title: UFO Reports and Atomic Sites
item.description:
Since 1947, US media has speculated about a link between UFO reports and atomic sites. Early in the Cold War, domestic UFO reports were taken seriously, with some media suggesting they were related to atomic energy transmutation, a claim later dismissed. By the 1990s, legends arose of UFOs interfering with missile tests and nuclear complexes. Statistical studies suggest UFO reports are more common near military or atomic sites, though experts argue this may be due to increased scrutiny of these areas.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_reports_and_atomic_sites

item.title: The Martian Monkey Hoax
item.description:
In 1953 Atlanta, Edward Watters, Tom Wilson, and Arnold Payne perpetrated a hoax using a dead rhesus monkey. They altered the monkey’s appearance and created scorch marks to suggest it was from a flying saucer. The prank occurred during a period of UFO hysteria, leading to public belief and an Air Force investigation. The hoax was exposed by an anatomy professor, and the perpetrators eventually confessed, with Watters paying a fine for obstructing a highway. The “Martian Monkey” is now displayed at the Georgia Bureau of Investigation headquarters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martian_Monkey

item.title: Gabriel L. 'Gabe' Valdez: Investigator of Cattle Mutilations
item.description:
Gabriel L. “Gabe” Valdez was a New Mexico State Police officer and Gaming Control Board investigator known for leading the criminal investigation into livestock mutilations in 1970s New Mexico. During his investigations, Valdez reported a mysterious aircraft and the discovery of tranquilizing and blood-removing drugs in mutilated animal remains. His son later stated that Valdez believed the mutilations were a government testing program, not alien activity. Valdez passed away on August 7, 2011.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gabe_Valdez

item.title: Unidentified Submerged Object
item.description:
An unidentified submerged object (USO) is an object that is unidentified and submerged in water. The U.S. Navy categorizes USOs as marine life that can disrupt naval operations. The term USO has also been used by ufologists to describe unidentified flying objects that may also travel underwater.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_submerged_object

item.title: Cardwell UFO Festival: A Mysterious Event in Australia
item.description:
The Cardwell UFO Festival, held annually in Cardwell, Queensland, is Australia’s sole UFO festival. Established in 2014, it serves as a platform to discuss local unusual sightings and activities. The festival features entertainment, family activities, markets, food, and an alien-themed costume party. It also includes “The C-Files,” a forum for attendees to share their UFO experiences and other mysterious sightings. In 2017, the festival was recognized as a Top 10 Festival for UFO and Alien Lovers and one of Australia’s 7 Most Eyebrow-Raising Festivals.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardwell_UFO_Festival

item.title: Tulli Papyrus: Claims and Controversies
item.description:
The Tulli Papyrus is purportedly an Egyptian papyrus from the reign of Thutmose III, with claims originating from Tiffany Thayer in the Fortean Society magazine Doubt. Boris de Rachewiltz claimed to have found a papyrus document describing “flying saucers” and provided a translation. However, Rachewiltz later stated he had not seen the papyrus himself and based his translation on notes taken by Alberto Tulli. Many, including ufologists and those involved in the Condon Report, have considered the papyrus to be a hoax, possibly plagiarized from other texts.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tulli_Papyrus

item.title: Vision Ovni
item.description:
Vision Ovni is an Argentine national UFO research organization founded in 1991. It began operations in Victoria, Entre Rios, in mid-1991, coinciding with press reports of strange lights from Laguna del Pescado lake. The organization’s objectives include researching UFO phenomena, collecting and studying information on UFOs in Argentina, and applying scientific principles to investigations. In 2009, Vision Ovni was part of the formation of Cefora, an Argentine organization dedicated to studying the UFO phenomena in detail.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visi%C3%B3n_Ovni

item.title: Igor Volke: Estonian Ufologist and Researcher of Environmental Anomalies
item.description:
Igor Volke was an Estonian ufologist and researcher of environmental anomalies, born in 1950 and passing away in 2024. In 1985, he founded AKRAK to collect reports of anomalous environmental phenomena, particularly UFO sightings, across Estonia. This organization was later succeeded by EUFON (Estonian UFO Network). Volke also authored several books on the subject, including “UFO Diaries.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AKRAK

item.title: Dr. Franklin Ruehl: American Actor, Ufologist, and Cryptozoologist
item.description:
Franklin Ruehl was an American actor, ufologist, and cryptozoologist. He hosted the public-access television show Mysteries from Beyond the Other Dominion, which later became the first Sci Fi Channel original series. Ruehl also appeared on various television programs, including Jimmy Kimmel Live! and Ancient Aliens, and hosted his own show, Professor Weird. He claimed to hold a Ph.D. in theoretical nuclear physics from UCLA. Ruehl died in November 2015.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franklin_Ruehl

item.title: Daniel Fry
item.description:
Daniel William Fry was an American contactee who claimed to have encountered aliens and ridden in a remotely piloted spacecraft. He founded the organization Understanding, Inc., which published a newsletter and spread alternative social and spiritual ideas. Fry’s claims were met with skepticism, and analysis of his photographic and film evidence suggested it was a hoax. He passed away in 1992.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Understanding%2C_Inc.

item.title: Chen Tao (UFO religion)
item.description:
Chen Tao, also known as God’s Salvation Church, was a UFO religion founded in Taiwan in 1996 by Hon-Ming Chen. The group relocated to Texas in 1997 and gained notoriety for a failed millennial prophecy. Chen Tao members wore white clothing and cowboy hats, and called their leader “Teacher Chen.” After their prophecy of God’s appearance did not occur, the group largely disbanded, with many members returning to Taiwan.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chen_Tao_%28UFO_religion%29

item.title: UFOetry in 2008, Joshua Poet on right
item.description:
UFOetry is a band from Los Angeles, California, formed in July 2004 by ufologist Joshua Poet. The band performs rock operas centered on UFO phenomena, combining live music and theatrical elements in their multimedia shows. They present their message at UFO festivals and conferences across the continental United States, aiming to spread awareness of UFO phenomena globally. UFOetry has received recognition from the Los Angeles music scene for various achievements, including music video and concert performance awards.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFOetry

item.title: Naga Fireballs: Mysterious Glowing Balls in the Mekong River
item.description:
Naga fireballs are a phenomenon observed annually on the Mekong River, where glowing, reddish balls are said to rise from the water and disappear into the air. These fireballs, ranging in size from sparkles to basketballs, are locally attributed to a giant serpent called the phaya nak. While the phenomenon is traditionally linked to the end of Vassa in late October, some observers suggest it may be caused by tracer rounds or flare guns fired from across the river. Scientific explanations have proposed flammable phosphine gas or plasma orbs, but these are debated.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naga_fireball

item.title: World Contact Day
item.description:
World Contact Day was initiated in March 1953 by the International Flying Saucer Bureau (IFSB) as a day for members to attempt telepathic communication with extraterrestrials. The IFSB theorized that a collective focus on a specific message could transmit it through space if telepathy and alien life were real. The message sent in 1953 invited occupants of interplanetary craft to make a peaceful appearance on Earth. The 1953 celebration inspired the song “Calling Occupants of Interplanetary Craft,” later covered by The Carpenters. In 2013, on its 60th anniversary, World Contact Day was expanded to a full week.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Contact_Day

item.title: UFOCOM or Belarusian UFOlogical Community
item.description:
The UFOCOM, or Belarusian Ufological Community, is a non-commercial, non-government public organization founded in 2001 in Horki, Belarus. It originated from students studying paranormal phenomena and has grown into a statewide network of volunteers conducting research. Since 2005, UFOCOM has been part of the Kosmopoisk international movement, serving as a public node for UFO research in the CIS network. The organization focuses on studying anomalous phenomena, including UFOs, crop circles, and poltergeists, and conducts research in cooperation with the National Academy of Sciences of Belarus.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UfoCom

item.title: Hessdalen lights
item.description:
The Hessdalen lights are unidentified lights observed in a valley in central Norway since at least the 1930s. These lights, which can appear day or night, vary in color, duration, and movement, sometimes moving at high speeds or hovering. Research projects, including “Project Hessdalen” and the Hessdalen Automatic Measurement Station, have been established to investigate the phenomenon. While some sightings have been attributed to misperceptions, theories involving combustion of airborne dust, dusty plasma, and piezoelectricity from rock strain have been proposed, though no definitive explanation has been reached.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hessdalen_lights

item.title: Wonsan-Sunchon UFO Incident
item.description:
On the night of January 29, 1952, during the Korean War, four American military personnel reported seeing an orange globe-shaped light over Wonsan and Sunchon. Air Force UFO experts believe this incident contributed to the 1952 UFO flap. The sightings occurred amid fears of new Soviet technology and during a stalemate in the war, with Wonsan under naval blockade and Sunchon experiencing a major air battle. The incident gained nationwide attention through United Press and was discussed in publications like Time and Life magazines, with the Air Force confirming an ongoing investigation.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wonsan-Sunchon_UFO_incident

item.title: Citizens Against UFO Secrecy
item.description:
Citizens Against UFO Secrecy (CAUS) was a non-profit organization founded in 1977 to advocate for the release of classified information regarding UFOs. The group used the Freedom of Information Act to challenge government secrecy surrounding UFO contact. Peter Gersten, a former director, filed lawsuits against the CIA and NSA, resulting in the release of some UFO documents. CAUS is now listed as an inactive organization.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citizens_Against_UFO_Secrecy

item.title: British Rail flying saucer patent
item.description:
The British Rail flying saucer, officially termed “space vehicle,” was a proposed interplanetary spacecraft designed by Charles Osmond Frederick. A patent application was filed for this craft by British Rail in December 1970 and granted in March 1973. The design, which evolved from a lifting platform to a large passenger craft, was to be powered by controlled thermonuclear fusion using laser beams. This concept was later examined by nuclear scientists who deemed it unworkable and inefficient, noting that many of the required technologies had not yet been discovered.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Rail_flying_saucer

item.title: Antrovis
item.description:
Antrovis, also known as the International Centre for the Restoration of Mankind and Earth, was a UFO religion that originated in Poland in the 1980s. Founded by Edward Mielnik, an energy medicine practitioner, the movement combined beliefs in Slavic nations, ufology, and Christianity, with influences from Helena Blavatsky and ancient astronaut theories. Antrovis was officially dissolved in 1994, but allegations of continued activity and recruitment persisted. The cult faced controversy due to its recruitment of young people and accusations of organ harvesting and involvement in disappearances.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antrovis

item.title: Paasselkä devils
item.description:
Paasselkä devils are light phenomena observed at Lake Paasselkä in Finland and its surrounding areas. These lights, often described as balls of light, can move at various speeds or remain stationary, and sometimes appear in multiples. Local folklore has long referred to these lights as “devils,” with some locals believing them to be conscious entities. The phenomenon, which has been observed for a long time and is part of local mythology, gained wider recognition through a 2006 book.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paasselk%C3%A4_devils

item.title: Above Majestic
item.description:
Above Majestic is a 2018 documentary film that proposes a global conspiracy theory involving reptilian aliens who secretly control world governments. The film posits that these aliens inhabit Antarctica, are preparing for an invasion, and are behind the September 11 attacks as a means to hide their conspiracy. According to Vice, the documentary claims these aliens control governments and banks. Researchers reported in 2021 that a QAnon-linked Twitter user may have aided in the film’s production.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Above_Majestic

item.title: Dancing Blue Lights at Silver Cliff Cemetery
item.description:
Silver Cliff Cemetery, established in the 1880s near Silver Cliff, Colorado, is divided into Catholic and Protestant sections. The cemetery is known for reports of “dancing blue lights” that have been observed since the 1880s. These lights, described as blue lantern lights or white spheres, were featured in National Geographic Magazine in August 1969. Researcher Karen Stollznow suggested natural explanations for the lights, such as visual phenomena related to the human eye.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silver_Cliff_Cemetery

item.title: Timothy Good
item.description:
Timothy Good is a British author who writes about UFOs, with his books including “Above Top Secret: The Worldwide U.F.O. Cover-up” (1987), “Alien Liaison” (1991), and “Beyond Top Secret” (1996). He has appeared on television and in documentaries, and his writings have been challenged by critics. In 1987, he was described as “Britain’s leading UFO researcher.” His 1987 book was cited by the CIA as a source contributing to the idea that the agency has secretly concealed its UFO research.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timothy_Good

item.title: Paulding Light
item.description:
The Paulding Light is a phenomenon observed in a valley near Paulding, Michigan, with reports dating back to the 1960s. Folklore has offered explanations such as ghosts, geological activity, or swamp gas. However, scientific investigations, including a 2010 study by Michigan Tech students, have concluded that the light is caused by automobile headlights and tail lights on a nearby stretch of US Highway 45. These students were able to recreate the effect by driving a car on that highway and observed vehicle lights through a telescope.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paulding_Light

item.title: Fund for UFO Research (FUFOR)
item.description:
The Fund for UFO Research (FUFOR) was a non-profit UFO research group founded in 1979 in Mount Rainier, Maryland. Its stated goals were to advance scholarly research into UFOs and the extraterrestrial hypothesis, and to facilitate the release of classified U.S. government documents related to these topics. From 1979 to 2006, FUFOR provided over $700,000 in research grants and supported investigations into subjects like the MJ-12 papers and Project Blue Book. The organization has not had an active online or print presence since 2011.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fund_for_UFO_Research

item.title: Gilliland's Ranch
item.description:
Gilliland’s Ranch, also known as ECETI Ranch and Sattva Sanctuary, is located in Trout Lake, Washington, at the base of Mount Adams. The property owner, James Gilliland, claims to have established contact with extraterrestrial intelligence and has hosted UFO sighting events since 2003. Gilliland reports frequent UFO sightings and “unexplained light shows” at the location.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gilliland%27s_Ranch

item.title: Norio Fernando Hayakawa
item.description:
Norio Hayakawa is an American activist born in Yokohama, Japan, in 1944. He is known for his UFOlogy investigations in New Mexico and the American Southwest. Hayakawa has appeared on Coast to Coast AM and was involved in organizing the Area 51 People’s Rally. He also led a Nippon TV crew in 1990 to investigate paranormal activity in Dulce, New Mexico.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norio_Hayakawa

item.title: Angel Hair and UFOs: A Mysterious Phenomenon Explained
item.description:
Angel hair, also known as siliceous cotton or Mary’s yarn, is a sticky, fibrous substance reported in connection with UFO sightings or manifestations of the Virgin Mary. It has been described as cobweb-like or jelly-like and often disintegrates or evaporates shortly after forming. While some theories suggest it is ionized air sleeting off a UFO’s electromagnetic field, many cases have been identified as the web threads of migrating spiders. Notable incidents of angel hair have been reported globally, including at Fatima in 1917 and in Oloron, France, in 1952.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angel_hair_%28folklore%29

item.title: Aerial Phenomena Research Organization
item.description:
The Aerial Phenomena Research Organization (APRO) was a UFO research group founded in January 1952. Based in Tucson, Arizona after 1960, APRO emphasized scientific investigations and employed consulting scientists, including James E. McDonald and James Harder. J. Allen Hynek considered APRO and NICAP to be the two most reputable civilian UFO groups of their time. A portion of APRO’s membership later formed the Mutual UFO Network (MUFON) in 1969. APRO’s case files were transferred to the National UFO Historical Records Center in November 2023.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aerial_Phenomena_Research_Organization

item.title: Green fireballs
item.description:
Green fireballs are a type of unidentified flying object reported since the early 1950s, primarily in the southwestern United States. Early reports from late 1948 described them as bright green balls of fire or like huge green meteors. While some ufologists suggest an extraterrestrial origin, mainstream explanations include natural bolides. Investigations in the late 1940s and early 1950s, including Project Twinkle, were unable to definitively identify the origin, with official conclusions leaning towards natural phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_fireballs

item.title: Civilian Saucer Investigation (CSI-LA)
item.description:
Civilian Saucer Investigation (CSI-LA) was a Los Angeles-based group that studied flying saucer and UFO reports in the early 1950s. The group was formed by three North American Aviation technical writers and a rocket scientist after they reported witnessing unusual aerial objects. CSI-LA was featured in Time and Life magazines and published a quarterly bulletin. The organization dissolved in October 1952 after one of its founders claimed the initial sighting was a joke.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civilian_Saucer_Investigation

item.title: America's Nuclear Flying Saucer
item.description:
The Lenticular Reentry Vehicle (LRV) was an experimental nuclear warhead delivery system developed during the Cold War by North American Aviation. Research into lenticular-shaped vehicles for re-entry characteristics began in the late 1950s, with the LRV project being classified in 1962 and declassified in 1999. The LRV was described as a 40-foot half-saucer capable of supporting a crew of four for six-week orbital missions, powered by a rocket engine and an onboard nuclear reactor. While its existence may lend credence to military flying saucer theories, its described flight characteristics are more akin to a standard space capsule.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenticular_Reentry_Vehicle

item.title: The Coming of the Saucers
item.description:
The Coming of the Saucers is a 1952 book by Kenneth Arnold and Raymond Palmer that expands on their earlier articles about unidentified flying objects. The book includes Arnold’s firsthand accounts of his sighting of flying disc-like shapes and his investigations into various UFO incidents. It also features third-person summaries of other UFO reports and is noted for introducing the concept of the “man in black.” The work has been cited as fueling the extraterrestrial hypothesis and influencing later UFO conspiracy theories.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Coming_of_the_Saucers

item.title: Jeff Peckman
item.description:
Jeff Peckman is a UFO disclosure activist from Denver, Colorado, born in 1954. He ran for the U.S. Senate in 1998 as a member of the Natural Law Party and later for Denver Mayor in 2011. Peckman has also been involved in ballot initiatives, including one in 2003 aimed at increasing peacefulness and a later initiative to create an Extraterrestrial Affairs Commission in Denver, which was rejected by voters. He gained media attention in 2008 for publicly displaying a video of a purported extraterrestrial.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeff_Peckman

item.title: CEFORA: Argentinean UFO Phenomenon Studies Committee
item.description:
CEFORA, the Argentinean Republic Committee for UFO Phenomena Studies, is an organization formed by various ufology groups in Argentina. Its primary objective is the declassification of all UFO-related phenomena in Argentina. CEFORA has formally requested information from the National Ministry of Defense, the National Presidency, and the Chamber of Deputies. Silvia Pérez Simondini serves as the director of CEFORA and also runs the Visión OVNI UFO Museum in Victoria, Entre Ríos.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cefora
